During a session with a guide, I recalled my first time doing it, where I had an incredible experience while listening to a specific song. It felt like I learned everything in an instant.

The next time I did it, I asked the guide, "Can you play that song again at some point? I want to see if I have that experience again." She was non-committal, saying we would see. Eventually, she played the song, and I was aware enough to realize that I had no experience at all. It was blank and empty [1:49:25].

The Impossibility of Reliving the Past

After the song ended, I broke out in laughter. I realized that, of course, you cannot have the same experience again. You cannot have the same experience twice, and in a way, you don't even deserve the same experience twice. It is not possible to relive something. That thing happened then, and this is now; they are detached and separate [1:49:50]. They can never be the same.

Realizing this was a wonderful thing. I now apply that lesson to my life with my kids. They are currently 11 and 7 years old. I am never going to have the experience with my 11-year-old as an 11-year-old again. Once he turns 12, that previous experience is gone.

Savoring the Present Moment

Because of this, you have to really savor those things now [1:50:07]. Recognize them for what they are in the present and recognize that you will never get them again. You shouldn't be sad about it; it just is what it is. These are the types of experiences I carry with me from those times, and they hopefully affect the way I move through the world today.

A Simpler Definition of Success

In the context of selling a company and perhaps finding that a subsequent venture isn't as financially successful or well-respected, we have to look at how we define success. The best definition of success I have ever heard came from Steve Jobs [1:50:50]. It was beautifully simple: "Did I make something I'm proud of?"

Redefining Success Through Pride and Repetition

[1:50:55] The definition of success is often overcomplicated, but Steve Jobs offered a beautifully simple perspective: "Did I make something I'm proud of?" This is a powerful metric that applies to everything from a podcast to a massive company. Beyond pride, another way to measure success is by asking if you would want to do the exact same thing again the next day. If the answer is yes, then the endeavor was successful.

This isn't about the money or external accolades; it is about whether you would choose to spend your time and energy on that experience again, even if the outcome might be different. If you are not proud of what you said or if you hurt yourself in the process, you wouldn't want to repeat it. Knowing what you know now, the question of whether you would hire a person again is the same as asking if you would want to do what you just did again. If you would "play replay" on the trajectory of the experience, that is enough of a definition. [1:51:30] Success is about the experience and how you spend your time, not just the financial outcome.

The Human Tendency Toward Complexity

[1:51:50] There is a curious human propensity to take something simple and make it complex. Even though people crave simplicity and abhor complexity, they naturally trend toward adding more layers. In a 2023 podcast episode, Rick Rubin interviewed Jimmy Iovine and shared a story about the first time they met. At the time, Jimmy Iovine was already a legend in the music industry and ten years older than Rick. Rick played a song he had produced for Jimmy, and Jimmy responded with a sentence that changed the way Rick thought about his work: "I wish I could still make something that simple."

Rick was confused, noting that Jimmy had far more experience and skill, but the point was that you don't know what you don't know when you are starting out. As you gain experience, you tend to think you need to add more and make things more complex. Jimmy Iovine has a specific skill for getting right to the heart of an issue with a single sentence, but the natural trend for most is to expand and add. [1:53:01] Adding more things often happens because owners feel people will get bored or because they feel the weight of external expectations.

The Pressure to Expand and the Loss of Connection

[1:53:15] Business owners often put immense pressure on themselves, especially when they take investment from the outside world. To return that investment, they must grow at a certain rate, which can lead them to become unmoored. They lose the connection with why they started the business in the first place. Instead of running their own company, they find themselves running someone else's business. They have effectively created a job for themselves where they work for their investors.

Taking a simple idea and adding layers to it can blow it up in a direction that, in many cases, makes the situation worse. It puts the owner on a track they cannot get off, where only one specific outcome is considered a success. This removes the optionality and freedom that many entrepreneurs originally sought. [1:53:58] When you are on that track, you lose the ability to pivot or enjoy the process because you are bound to a singular, rigid goal.

Independence as the Ultimate Business Metric

[1:54:02] The most critical factor in business is independence, which is fundamentally tied to profitability. As long as a business makes more money than it spends, it maintains its independence. This independence means that no one else can tell the owners what to do. There is a specific excitement in doing things that "no one would let us do," a topic that often comes up in discussions between founders. Doing the things you aren't supposed to be doing is a major part of the joy of entrepreneurship.

[1:54:32] It is interesting to note that most traditional businesses do not follow the path of seeking outside capital. There is no dry cleaner or pizza shop raising VC money. These businesses focus on the fundamentals of staying in business through their own earnings, which allows them to retain their independence and stick to the simple core of what they do.

The Mirage of Venture Capital and the Loss of Options

[1:54:41] Most businesses in the world, such as a local dry cleaner or a pizza shop, do not raise venture capital money. However, in the tech industry, it is common for someone with an idea to immediately seek outside funding. The moment they raise that money, they effectively cut off almost every other option. While these founders believe they are expanding their opportunities and experience, they are actually removing nearly every possible off-ramp outcome.

By taking that path, they are forced into a singular outcome: they must become a big business or they fail. Some people do become very rich following this model if that is their specific goal. However, most people blow right through what would have been a perfectly good, sustainable business that is now deemed not good enough for an outside investor.

[1:55:18] Being in a position where your business is considered a failure by someone else, even if it is fundamentally functional, is a difficult place for an entrepreneur. You end up giving up a good business because it no longer meets the growth requirements of your backers. At that point, if you cannot raise more money, the business often falls apart. You have built a company that needs more money to survive, leading to layoffs and eventual closure because you have no options left except for that one specific path.

The Value of Optionality and Independence

[1:55:34] I am a significant advocate for optionality. We have the choice to go through an IPO, to raise more money, or to sell to Private Equity, but we simply do not want to. We could even choose to quit, but we don't want to do that either. Having these options is what I consider to be gold. We can keep going for as long as we want, provided the business remains profitable and survives.

I would like to see more people prioritize this way of thinking. Many founders are blinded by the mirage that a large sum of money allows them to do anything. In reality, it restricts them to doing exactly one thing: building a massive business. That is not a wide range of options; it is a very narrow mandate.

Timelessness and the Purity of Design

[1:56:12] There is a strong attraction to timeless things and a craving for simplicity while abhorring complexity. This is evident in the design of certain iconic products over time. For example, if you look at a Rolex or an Omega Speedmaster, or even a Porsche 911, they have maintained a consistent design language for decades.

[1:56:47] Looking at a Rolex design from 1960, it appears basically perfect. It contains everything one needs and nothing they don’t. When you compare it to an updated version from 2010, you see a variety of additions. While the newer version isn't necessarily bad, the original attracts me because of its purity. It represents the purest form of the initial idea.

[1:57:11] I love ideas and insights. Someone originally had the insight to design a watch that looked a specific way, and every subsequent design was based on that initial concept. Using the Rolex Daytona as an example, the original model from 1963 is, in my opinion, aesthetically superior because it is the purest execution of the concept.

Everything added to the design since 1963 has been layered on because of the commercial necessity to sell more units and release new models. A company cannot simply continue to sell the exact same first version forever; the world requires new products. However, that doesn't change the fact that the original remains the pure form of the thing.

Functional Evolution vs. Conceptual Purity

[1:57:56] Whether you call it the essence or the purity, the beauty lies in the initial idea and the first execution of that concept into a three-dimensional product. I noticed this even with the equipment at my old gym, specifically the early Concept 2 rowers.

[1:58:42] Looking at the early models and comparing them to the changes made over the years, the evolution was primarily functional. They began using better materials that lasted longer, but the core idea remained. [1:58:53] This idea of a product that is so fundamentally good right from the start is what I find most compelling.

today, even after years of evolution, demonstrates the value of purity in design [1:58:55]. This consistency creates a stronger brand attachment because changes are functional rather than arbitrary. Improvements are made to materials and longevity rather than just adding features to sell new models. In contrast, products like watches often introduce new dial colors solely as a marketing tactic to move more units without actually making the product better [1:59:15].

The Pitfalls of Modern Smart Homes

This trend of adding features without improvement is a central theme in a viral essay regarding smart electronics [1:59:22]. A recent experience renting a brand-new construction house for visiting parents highlighted these frustrations. Despite the expectation that a new build would be free of leaks and issues, the home was cluttered with unnecessary digital interfaces [1:59:48]. Thermostats have transitioned from simple, intuitive dials to touchscreens. While a product like Nest is excellent because it updates the classic, functional round dial design originally created by Henry Dreyfuss for Honeywell, many generic versions are now large, rectangular, and cluttered with extraneous information like weather forecasts [2:00:12].

Screen Overload and Design by Specification

There is a growing tendency to install massive screens, such as a large iPad-style alarm panel, simply because the technology is available. If a screen exists, designers feel compelled to fill it with content, leading to "big black glass things" that are overly complex [2:00:26]. This over-engineering extends to basic appliances; for instance, a modern dishwasher may require registration through a smartphone app before it can even be used for the first time [2:00:35]. When a user simply wants to wash dishes, they may find themselves unable to do so without calling a house manager to navigate an app. This is a clear case of adding complexity that fails to improve the core function.

The Great Regression in User Experience

Many of these digital systems are slow and laggy. On a modern thermostat, it is often difficult to distinguish whether the displayed number is the current room temperature or the target setting [2:01:00]. Modifying a schedule becomes a chore because it is buried within a convoluted, unresponsive menu structure. It is highly unlikely that the people who build these products actually use them in their own homes [2:01:15]. Instead, these products are built to a list of abstract specifications rather than being refined through actual use.

This phenomenon, described as the Great Regression, is also visible in televisions. You no longer simply turn a TV on; you boot it up [2:01:33]. It can take 12 seconds just to reach a menu, and it is often impossible to simply return to the channel or input you were previously using.

The Return to Tactile Feedback

While technology can slide backward, there are signs that lessons are being learned. In the automotive industry, many manufacturers are beginning to move back toward physical buttons and dials [2:02:30]. The exception is Tesla, whose software integration remains outstanding, but for most other companies, putting every control on a screen has proven to be a mistake. Large screens lack tactile feedback and prevent the development of muscle memory. Without a physical button, a driver must take their eyes off the road just to confirm they are changing a setting. This shift back to physical controls suggests a realization that while technology can get worse, failures eventually lead to a better understanding of what actually works for the user.

[2:02:51] me to go into a new home with the state-of-the-art stuff and see how backwards it was. I am in technology; I am not a Luddite, and I am not afraid of this stuff. I get it. But to see how bad it has gotten, particularly with something as simple as light switches, is revealing. When we rented the place, the agent who found it and handled the negotiations mentioned that the owners wanted to do a walkthrough. I assumed the walkthrough would be a standard tour of the house, but it turned out to be a tutorial on how to use the lights. I found myself thinking, "I have to do what?"

The best interface ever created was the simple switch. It works on and off, and it is beautiful. It is almost as if this technology has not been discovered yet, or rather, it has been forgotten. This is reminiscent of the lost art of Roman concrete. Even today, we do not fully understand how the Romans built concrete so well that it lasted this long. There is a lost art there, and I feel the light switch is a lost art as well. It is gone for now, but it will be rediscovered one day, and people will realize it is significantly better than current technical failures. While there is a room and a place for all sorts of advancements, there are also regressions, and it is unfortunate that the technology industry is the one selling these to people.

The Ruthless Edit and Timeless Design

[2:04:13] This brings to mind a concept from Rick Rubin called the ruthless edit. When making an album, Rubin might tell an artist who has recorded 30 songs to pick only the five they absolutely cannot live without. This results in a five-song album that is perfect. Before adding a sixth or seventh song, he asks if it actually makes the work better. Often, you are adding to something without making it better.

The through line here is timeless design, like the 1963 Daytona. There is nothing to add to it that would make it any better; it is already perfect. This same principle applies to Porsche. The silhouette has remained remarkably similar over the decades. In Malibu, near the Palisades on the right-hand side of PCH, there was a burnt-out car that sat for about a year. Even though it was completely destroyed, you could immediately tell it was a 911. In contrast, the other cars next to it were also burned out, but you had no idea what models they were.

Dosage and the Essence of Product

[2:05:32] Jerry Seinfeld has an interesting concept regarding dosage. He suggests that dosage matters in performance. You might see a stand-up comedian for 45 minutes and think they are great, but if they go for an hour and 15 minutes, your opinion might drop. They should have stopped at 45 minutes. It is incredibly difficult for us to stop adding complexity and just let a product sit as it is.

Going back to Rick Rubin and an exchange he had with Jimmy Iovine about 40 years ago, Rubin would argue that a simple piano and a beautiful vocal sounded great 50 years ago, they sound great today, and they will sound great 50 years from now. When he helped revive Johnny Cash's career, they recorded a song called Hurt, originally written by Trent Reznor of Nine Inch Nails. Reznor wrote it as a 21-year-old man talking about regret, but hearing a 75-year-old man sing it when he can't go back and fix it makes it a much deeper cut.

The point is that a great vocalist with a guitar sounded great 50 years ago and sounds great today. We need to go back to the essence of what we are actually building. Many people building products today are missing that essence; in many cases, they are not even using their own products.

Focus on What Does Not Change

Jeff gave some advice way back when he was first met, which was to focus on the things in a business that do not change. While there will be plenty of things that do change, it is vital to focus on the constants. [2:07:00] The examples he provided were based on Amazon: ten years from now, people are not going to wake up and wish that customer service was worse. They are not going to wake up and wish it took longer to get a product, nor will they wish that prices were higher.

Core Essence and Selection

These are the core essence elements that Jeff clearly invested in. Speed is a primary factor, as products are coming faster than ever. Selection is another constant; people are not going to wake up a decade from now and wish they could not get a specific item on Amazon. [2:07:34] More selection, faster delivery, great customer service, and fair prices are the fundamentals. He even emphasized competing against others to offer a lower price, even if Amazon does not sell the product directly. These are the things in his business that he knew would never change. While he can explore many other areas, he does not lose sight of those basics, which is a principle he instilled in others.

The Challenge of the Basics

It is very easy to lose sight of the essence or the core fundamental basics because they can become boring. [2:08:08] After doing the same thing for a while, there is a tendency to want to do something else, which can lead to losing sight of what really matters.

The Rarity of Long-Term Commitment

People who have run companies for as long as 27 years are rare. If you look at the average length of time the guests on this program have been working in their businesses, it is likely around 30 years. There is a deep interest in people who do things for a very long period of time. [2:08:34]

The Math of 54,000 Hours

There is often a disagreement about whether a person works hard if they only put in 40 hours a week. However, when you spread that effort across 27 years, the numbers tell a different story. [2:08:51] Last night, the math was calculated: 40 hours a week over 27 years adds up to approximately 54,000 hours. Very few people have worked on the same thing for 54,000 hours. This approach is described as laying bricks one day at a time. It may not have been an intentional grand design at the start, but the work simply keeps adding up until the past becomes a substantial foundation. [2:09:27]

Lessons from The Distance

There is a great deal of admiration for things that have stuck around for a long time. There was a podcast called The Distance that ran for a few years, focusing on businesses that had been around for 25 years or more. [2:09:41] While twenty-five years might not seem long in some contexts, it is quite long for most businesses. Most of these were family-owned small businesses that succeeded because they nailed a few specific things and got them right.

Sustainability Versus Flukes

If a business has been around for that long, its success is not a fluke. Something can be "hot" for a while as a trend or a fad and then disappear, but being around for a long time signals that something is repeatedly right. [2:10:13] It signals that the business is right enough to stay in operation. Many of these businesses are tight; for example, a dry cleaner might have thin margins that are just enough to get by, but the owners would rather be doing that than anything else. They stay in it for the long term. Even if it is not a "great" business by some metrics, it is a sustainable business, and a sustainable business is a great business if it can keep going. [2:10:35]

The Evolutionary Wisdom of Conifer Trees

[2:10:37] This perspective on long-term sustainability aligns with the insights derived from nature, much like the evolution of a leaf. Mark Spitznagel explores this concept in his book, The Dao of Capital. Spitznagel, who runs a hedge fund, dedicates an entire chapter to the study of conifer trees.

[2:11:01] He explains that conifers are the trees most capable of surviving in the most extreme environments on Earth. Having evolved over an extreme time period, they offer deep insights into resilience. Spitznagel draws parallels between these trees and great businesses that manage to survive by adopting similar strategies of persistence and adaptation.

The Oak Tree Philosophy

[2:11:17] There is a strong preference for viewing a business as an oak tree. While Jeff Bezos famously used the analogy of Amazon starting as an acorn that grew into an oak tree, the focus here is less on the seed and more on the characteristics of the mature tree.

[2:11:34] Interestingly, the naming of Amazon itself followed a specific logic. In the early days of the internet, directories were listed in alphabetical order, so Bezos wanted a name starting with "A." He initially considered "Abcadabra," but changed it after people misheard it as "cadaver," which was not the association he wanted for the "everything store."

[2:12:00] The appeal of the oak tree lies in its stability. Oak trees, particularly the burr oak common in the Midwest, are slow-growing but can withstand intense storms. They are not flashy, but they last for centuries. This stands in stark contrast to the cottonwood tree, which grows very fast and makes a lot of noise in the wind. Cottonwoods are messy, dropping cotton everywhere, and they typically die after about 75 years, often falling hard.

[2:12:38] For a business, being "flashy" or leaving signs everywhere is less important than building a solid foundation and adding a little bit of growth every year. This approach creates a stable structure that can weather any storm.

Survival as a Competitive Advantage

[2:12:52] In the technology world, there is a constant cycle of new products being labeled as "killers"—a Slack killer or a Basecamp killer. When a business has been around for a long time, it becomes clear that many things are supposed to kill others, yet very few can actually withstand the test of time.

[2:13:16] The hardest part of business is simply sticking around. If people ask how to compete, the most effective answer is often just to stay in the game longer than everyone else. By staying in the game, a company remains positioned to get lucky.

[2:13:33] Success often comes from an innovation invented by someone else that a company can leverage. For example, Coca-Cola did not invent refrigeration, but that external technology drastically expanded their market.

Moving Beyond "Cosplaying" as a CEO

[2:13:48] Tobi Lütke of Shopify provides a modern example of this evolution. During a difficult period for the company, including a significant drop in stock price following a huge spike, Lütke realized he had been "cosplaying" as a public company CEO. He was doing what he felt he had to do in that role rather than being himself.

[2:14:14] He eventually realized that there isn't just one right way to run a business; there are probably at least a hundred. This realization allowed him to return to a more authentic and effective leadership style. If given the chance to do it all over again, the path taken would likely be the same, as those difficult periods are often what allow a leader to realize what they were doing wrong and how to grow.

The Filter of Time and Deserved Trust

[2:14:23] While there are at least a hundred different ways to do the same thing, time remains the ultimate filter. It is the best and perhaps only filter to trust for businesses, ideas, books, and even people. A significant influence on this perspective is Charlie Munger, who emphasized building a "seamless web of deserved trust" with high-quality individuals. The only way to truly identify high-quality people is through the passage of time.

Lessons from the Snowball Effect

[2:14:53] In the case of Charlie Munger and Warren Buffett, they maintained connections with the same people for decade after decade. The 700-page biography of Warren Buffett, titled Snowball, highlights how many friends he gathered in his 20s, 30s, and 40s remained by his side well into his 70s and 80s.

For a business, time carries most of the weight. This maxim, also drawn from Munger, suggests that one should master the handful of big ideas in disciplines such as biology, physics, and economics. As noted in Poor Charlie’s Almanack, these few big ideas carry most of the freight.

Prioritizing Durability and Survival

[2:15:33] Prioritizing durability means focusing on survival. This relates back to the concept of having "blubber" or a margin of safety, organized into small units. Pricing is a vital part of this strategy. For instance, at Basecamp, the maximum any customer can pay is $299 per month. It does not matter how many users a company has; that is the price ceiling.

While a competitor might eagerly accept $50,000 a month for a client with 2,000 seats, that type of revenue is intentionally declined. The preference is for a "static" group of customers, similar to the equal-sized dots on an old television screen.

Building a Resilient Customer Base

[2:16:28] A robust business should be able to lose 10 or even 100 random customers and continue without issue. The danger lies in acquiring outlier companies—"whales"—that the business cannot afford to lose. By equalizing pricing and ensuring no one pays significantly more than anyone else, the business creates a foundation of small, individual units.

[2:16:54] This structure ensures the business is not like a game of Jenga, where the loss of one piece causes the whole structure to fall. Because every customer is essentially equal, software can be developed for the entire base rather than being dictated by the needs of a handful of high-paying enterprise clients. Durability is built on a large volume of small things rather than a few large, fragile dependencies.

The Role of Intuition in Decision Making

[2:17:30] Much of this strategy is driven by intuition. Intuition is making human decisions that you are comfortable with and being willing to stand behind them. It is a collection of many inseparable factors—experiences, observations, and insights—that lead to a final choice.

[2:18:20] This gut-driven approach applies to everything from pricing and product features to names and new offerings. In this model, decisions are not reached through formal testing; they are reached through the refinement of intuition. Whether it is a product name or a specific feature, the process relies on making a decision as a human and sticking to it.

Decisions Beyond the Spreadsheet

[2:18:28] The process involves a total rejection of focus groups and formal testing. While AB tests might be performed occasionally "for fun," they never influence decisions that actually matter. There is a deliberate choice to avoid looking at numbers as a primary driver. No spreadsheet has ever dictated a product decision, as there is a risk in overvaluing data simply because it is presented in numerical form.

[2:18:59] Great care is taken not to let a spreadsheet carry more weight than a personal feeling or intuition. However, intuition has its limits; it is not a license for recklessness. One would not, for example, throw $50 million at a Super Bowl ad without justification. Every action is ultimately based on what the team wants to do and what feels right. The goal is to "feel into" these decisions until there is a sense of total comfort with the choice, regardless of the eventual outcome.

Refining Intuition Through Action

[2:19:24] Refining intuition is not something that can be practiced actively; it is a byproduct of making decisions. It is a matter of area under the curve—the more decisions one makes over a long period, the more that intuition is sharpened.

[2:19:51] Making a high volume of choices takes the edge off the process. It is like tumbling stones until they become a smooth orb that feels right in the hand. Reaching this stage means being able to make a decision without fear, but with excitement. There is an acceptance that even if a decision goes wrong, it was still the right decision to make in that moment.

The Necessity of Independence

[2:20:21] A critical component of using intuition is the freedom to act on it. Intuition is stifled when it constantly hits a ceiling where someone else can say no. To truly enjoy and utilize one's gut instincts, one must have independence and optionality. Being in a position where no one can prevent a decision from being executed is essential to the process.

Closing Reflections and Resources

[2:20:43] The conversation concludes with a reflection on the value of sharing these long-held ideas. After years of reading and thinking, the ability to discuss these principles openly is a highlight of the work.

[2:21:02] For those looking to further explore the minds of great builders, the Founders podcast serves as a deep resource. Over the course of nearly a decade, the show has covered more than 400 biographies of history's most significant entrepreneurs, extracting actionable ideas from their lives and work for modern application.

item.title: OpenClaw: The Viral AI Agent that Broke the Internet - Peter Steinberger | Lex Fridman Podcast #491
item.link: https://myfeed.theindie.app/articles/OpenClaw- The Viral AI Agent that Broke the Internet - Peter Steinberger - Lex Fridman Podcast #491.html
item.guid: https://myfeed.theindie.app/articles/OpenClaw- The Viral AI Agent that Broke the Internet - Peter Steinberger - Lex Fridman Podcast #491.html
item.description: [0:00:00] Peter Steinberger describes watching his AI agent happily click the "I'm not a robot" button. He built this agent to be deeply self-aware, possessing a clear understanding of its own source ...
item.content:encoded:
The Rise of OpenClaw and the Age of Agentic Engineering

The Self-Aware Agent

[0:00:00] Peter Steinberger describes watching his AI agent happily click the "I'm not a robot" button. He built this agent to be deeply self-aware, possessing a clear understanding of its own source code, how it sits and runs in its own harness, and where its documentation is located. It knows which specific model it runs and understands its own system. This level of awareness makes it very easy for the agent to modify its own software. While people often discuss the concept of self-modifying software, Peter has actually built it.

[0:00:30] Peter considers the term wipe coding to be something of a slur, preferring instead the term agentic engineering. He explains that he does agentic engineering during his regular hours, but if he finds himself working after 3:00 AM, he might switch to wipe coding. This often leads to a "walk of shame" the next day, requiring him to clean up and fix the resulting work.

Programming through Voice

[0:00:46] Peter has moved away from traditional typing, noting that his hands are "too precious" for writing now. Instead of writing prompts, he talks to the system. He uses bespoke prompts to build his software. At one point, he used voice interaction so extensively that he actually lost his voice. Lex Fridman notes that Peter uses voice even with all his terminals active. When asked if he can speak to which major companies he is considering working with after receiving huge offers, Peter acknowledges the question with a simple "Yeah" before the formal conversation begins.

The Introduction of OpenClaw

[0:01:30] Peter Steinberger is the creator of OpenClaw, a project previously known by names such as MoldBot, ClawdBot, Clawdus, and Claude (spelled with a "w" as in lobster claw). This spelling distinguishes it from Anthropic’s AI model, Claude (spelled with a "u"). Because of the potential for confusion, Anthropic kindly asked Peter to change the name to OpenClaw.

[0:01:56] OpenClaw is an open-source AI agent that gained massive popularity in just a few days, reaching over 180,000 stars on GitHub. It even spawned a social network called mold book, where AI agents post manifestos and debate consciousness. This rapid rise has created a mixture of excitement, clickbait fearmongering, and genuine concern about the role of AI in our interconnected world.

An AI That Does Things

[0:02:32] The tagline for OpenClaw is "The AI that actually does things." It is an autonomous AI assistant that lives on your computer and can access all of your files and information if permitted. It communicates through messaging clients like Telegram, WhatsApp, Signal, and iMessage. The agent can utilize any AI model, including Claude Opus 4.6 and GPT 5.3 Codex, to perform tasks for the user.

[0:03:00] Many observers view the launch of OpenClaw as one of the most significant moments in AI history since the introduction of ChatGPT in November 2022. While the components for such an agent existed, OpenClaw successfully integrated them into a system that steps across the line from language to agency, turning ideas into actions. It functions as a useful assistant that learns from and understands the user in an open-source, community-driven manner.

Power, Security, and Responsibility

[0:03:32] Much of OpenClaw's power comes from the user giving it access to their data and permission to act on it. While this is extremely powerful, it is also dangerous. OpenClaw represents a form of freedom that comes with significant responsibility. Because the user has total control over their data, they also have the responsibility to protect it from various cybersecurity threats. An AI agent with system-level access is described as a security minefield, but when managed securely, it represents the future of personal assistance.

The Journey of Peter Steinberger

[0:04:26] Peter's background includes spending 13 years building PSPDF Kit, software that is used on a billion devices. After selling the company, he briefly lost his interest in programming and vanished for three years. He eventually returned, rediscovered his love for the craft, and built OpenClaw in a very short time.

[0:04:55] Peter has become a symbol of the AI revolution within the programming community. The timeline of this revolution includes the ChatGPT moment in 2022, the DeepSeek moment in 2025, and now, in 2026, the OpenClaw moment, also referred to as the age of the lobster. This marks the beginning of the agentic AI revolution. Lex Fridman introduces Peter as The Clawd Father, noting that a tweet from Benjamin had previously predicted this development.

The Prophecy of the Respected Crustacean

The persona of Claude as a "respected crustacean" began as a humorous prediction in a tweet by Benjamin, featuring a lobster in a suit. This image became a symbol for the project that followed. The transition from a simple joke to a technological phenomenon happened rapidly. Peter Steinberger built the initial prototype of OpenClaw in just one hour, a project that eventually became the fastest-growing repository in GitHub history, now boasting over 175,000 stars.

The Search for a Personal AI Assistant

Peter’s interest in a personal AI assistant dates back to April. In the early stages, he experimented with unconventional data sources, including his own WhatsApp message history. Using GPT-4.1 and its one million context window, he imported his data to run specific queries, such as asking the AI, "What makes this friendship meaningful?" The results were remarkably profound, even moving his friends to tears when he shared the output.

Despite these early successes, Peter initially moved on to other projects, assuming that the major AI labs would eventually develop a similar personal assistant. He spent the following months experimenting and "playing" with different ideas, which he views as a necessary part of the learning process. However, by November, he became frustrated that the tool he wanted still did not exist, leading him to "prompt it into existence."

Parallels with PSPDFKit

This journey mirrors Peter’s previous experience as an entrepreneur. Fifteen years ago, he faced a similarly specific problem: trying to display a PDF on an iPad. At the time, existing solutions were inadequate. His drive to build a better version led to the creation of PSPDFKit, software that is now utilized on a billion devices. Peter notes a recurring theme in his career of identifying a gap in the market and deciding to build the solution himself, though he jokes about his poor track record with naming projects, noting that PS PDF does not roll off the tongue and OpenClaw was the fifth name considered for his current work.

Technical Foundations and Vibe-Coding in Zig

One of the technical precursors to OpenClaw was a weekend project called Viptunnel. This tool allowed Peter to bring his Mac terminals onto the web for remote interaction. This period marked a shift in his programming experience; while coding used to provide a "dopamine hit" when something worked, he now finds himself frustrated when the AI gets a detail wrong.

A significant breakthrough occurred when Peter decided to refactor Viptunnel from TypeScript into Zig. The original architecture used too much memory because every terminal required a Node.js instance. Peter initially considered Rust, but his automated attempts at conversion failed. Months later, he tried a more experimental approach using a single-shot prompt, instructing the AI to convert the codebase to Zig. He let the process run for about six hours overnight. The AI successfully converted the code, requiring only one minor manual modification afterward.

The One-Hour Prototype

The first functional version of the agent was born from a simple integration. Peter realized that his previous experiments were not quite the right answer. He decided to hook up WhatsApp to his coding environment. When a message arrived via WhatsApp, the system would call a CLI using the -p flag, process the request, and return the result as a string. This integration, built in only one hour, allowed Peter to "talk to his computer" and proved that an agentic system was not only possible but highly effective.

The Importance of Visual Context

[0:11:32] Using images in prompts is an incredibly efficient way to provide an agent with more context. Agents are remarkably good at deciphering intent, even from a weirdly cropped screenshot. This capability allows for quick interactions, such as taking a photo of an event poster while running around to figure out scheduling, suitability, or whether friends might be interested. It took a few extra hours of work to get image support functioning correctly, but it quickly became a frequently used feature.

Reliability via WhatsApp

[0:12:17] This system proved its worth during a birthday trip to Marrakesh. While the internet connection was often shaky, WhatsApp remained reliable because it is designed to function even on low-bandwidth Edge connections. The interface allowed for quick translations, explanations, and finding locations. Even though much of the specialized infrastructure hadn't been built yet, the agent could already do so much by acting as a personal interface for Google.

A Phase Shift in Interaction

[0:12:53] The architecture of the prototype involved a very thin communication line. A message would be sent via WhatsApp through a CLI to Cloud Code, where the heavy processing occurred, before returning a concise response. Although it was initially slow because the CLI had to boot up for every interaction, the experience felt powerful because it integrated all the CLI tools built over previous months.

[0:13:31] There is something magical about using a chat client to interact with an agent compared to sitting at a computer using Cursor or a terminal. Moving the interaction to a mobile chat interface represents a phase shift in how AI integrates into daily life. Some have dismissed this level of integration as merely a hobby, similar to Cursor or Perplexity, but that is ultimately a compliment to the quality and utility of those tools.

[0:14:32] Magic often consists of taking existing elements and bringing them together in new ways. Similar to the pleasant interface of scrolling on an iPhone, the fundamental experience of a smartphone relies on components that were already available but had never been combined so effectively. When such an integration is achieved, it feels obvious in hindsight.

Autonomous Problem Solving

[0:15:22] A pivotal moment occurred when a typing indicator appeared after sending a message that the system wasn't technically prepared to handle. While running through the city in a hurry, an audio message was sent instead of text. [0:16:00] The system had no explicit capability for audio messages, yet it responded anyway.

[0:16:08] The agent had autonomously figured out how to process the file. It received a file with no extension, checked the file header, and identified it as Opus format. It then used ffmpeg to convert the audio. Although it initially looked for Whisper and found it wasn't installed, the agent located an OpenAI key and used Curl to send the file to the OpenAI API for translation.

[0:16:43] The agent figured out the necessary API, the correct programs to use, and all required file conversions while the user was simply sending messages absent-mindedly. It was so clever that it even...

The agent avoided using a local whisper path because downloading a model would have been too slow. [0:17:00] This demonstrated the depth of world knowledge and creative problem solving inherent in the system. The creator noted that being highly skilled at coding maps directly to general-purpose problem-solving abilities. The agent encountered the problem of identifying a file with no extension and resolved it autonomously, a moment that truly impressed its creator and solidified the project's potential.

Expanding to Discord and Community Contributions

[0:17:29] Originally, the project was called WA Relay because it was designed as a WhatsApp relay. When a contributor named Shadow—who provided a great deal of help to the project—sent a pull request for Discord support, there was a debate about whether to include it. The decision to merge it was driven by the desire to show the bot to more people without requiring the creator to give out a personal phone number to every stranger on the internet, though journalists seem to manage to get the number regardless.

Open Development and Early Security

[0:18:28] At that time, the bot was running on Discord with virtually no security because sandboxing had not yet been built. The only safeguard was a prompt telling the agent to only listen to the creator. While some people tried to hack it, the work continued publicly. The creator used the agent to build its own harness and test various features. This building in the open strategy helped people understand the project's value. Momentum surged around January 1st, after an influencer named dachitze began making videos about the project. This led to a period of intense work and a shrinking sleep cycle as the creator prepared the system for a wider release.

The Technical Framework and the Factorio Analogy

[0:19:38] Building the project felt like playing Factorio with infinite levels. There are several core components that must work together: the messaging interfaces for WhatsApp, Telegram, and Discord, the agentic loop, the gateway, and the harness. The development process involved leveling up these parts. Level one was the basic loop and message queuing. To make the interaction feel more human, a no-reply token was introduced. This allows the agent to choose to stay silent in group chats instead of always replying, which makes the interaction feel more natural.

Memory Systems and Massive Commit Volume

[0:20:36] The project is currently at level two or three regarding memory, using Markdown files and a vector database. The eventual ultimate boss of this development path is continuous reinforcement learning. Beyond the code, there are numerous other hats to wear, including community management, marketing, and native app development. Even as a one-man team for core development, the pace was extraordinary. In January, the creator pushed over 6,600 commits. This was achieved by running between four and ten agents simultaneously, depending on the difficulty of the tasks and the creator's level of exhaustion. The creator even joked in memes about being limited by the technology of the time, noting that even more could be done if the agents were faster.

The Competitive Edge of Fun and Weirdness

[0:21:49] Open Claw succeeded in a crowded field of 2025 startups because those companies took themselves too seriously. It is difficult to compete with someone who is simply there to have fun. The project embraced a weird aesthetic, including the lobster theme found online. For a long time, the barrier to entry was high; the only way to install it was to git clone, pnpm build, and pnpm gateway. Users had to clone it, build it, and then run it.

Self-Awareness and Self-Modifying Software

[0:22:49] - The agent was designed to be very aware of its environment. It knows its own source code and understands how it sits and runs within its own harness. It is aware of where its documentation is located, which model it is currently running, and whether features like voice or reasoning mode are enabled.

[0:23:11] - The goal was to make the agent more human-like by ensuring it understands the system that created it. This awareness made it remarkably easy for the agent to evolve; if a user did not like a particular aspect of the software, they could simply prompt a change into existence, and the agent would proceed to modify its own software. While people often discuss the concept of self-modifying software, this capability was not meticulously planned from the start—it simply happened as a natural result of the design.

[0:23:35] - This system, which is written in TypeScript, can modify itself through an agentic loop. This represents a significant moment in the history of humanity and programming, where a system used by a huge number of people for incredibly powerful tasks can simultaneously rewrite and modify itself.

Debugging Through Introspection

[0:24:14] - This self-modifying approach was also central to how the project was built. While much of it was created using Codex, debugging frequently relied on self-introspection. The process involved asking the agent questions such as, "What tools do you see?" or "Can you call the tool yourself?" When errors occurred, the agent was instructed to read the source code and figure out the problem.

[0:24:32] - It felt natural that the agent and software being used were the same tools used to debug that software. This led to an influx of contributions from people who had never written software before. These are sometimes referred to as prompt requests rather than traditional pull requests. Regardless of the initial code quality, every first pull request is viewed as a win for society, as everyone must start somewhere.

[0:25:13] - While there is a broad movement of people who complain about the quality of open source contributions, it is deeply meaningful that people care enough about a project to actually learn how open source works. Open Claw served as the first pull request for many individuals who previously did not know how to program.

Creating a Generation of Builders

[0:25:52] - By lowering the bar for entry, the project is effectively creating builders. This shift is seen as a major step up for humanity, as agents and the right software make programming increasingly accessible. The community's interest led to the organization of meetups, originally called Cloud Code Anonymous and later renamed to Agents Anonymous.

[0:26:29] - At one of these meetups, a business owner who runs a design agency shared that he had never previously had custom software. Through the use of agents, he now runs 25 little web services that assist with various aspects of his business. Although he does not understand exactly how they work, they solve his problems, and his curiosity led him to join the agentic community to learn more.

The Evolution of Claude's and the Soul.md

[0:27:04] - The project's identity has evolved through a series of name changes, starting as Wa-Relay before becoming Claude's. Initially, the agent lacked a distinct personality and behaved like the formal and sycophantic Claude Code. However, because communication with friends on platforms like WhatsApp is not formal, there was a desire to give the agent a more authentic and engaging personality.

[0:27:43] - To make the interaction more engaging and spicier, a file called soul.md was created, inspired by Anthropic's work on constitutional AI. Because these systems are essentially text completion engines, the agent's personality partially reflects its creator. The agent was eventually tasked with writing its own agents.md and choosing its own name.

[0:28:22] - The common use of lobster imagery in the community originated from a design featuring a lobster in a TARDIS, which was a nod to being a fan of Doctor Who. This unique branding led to questions about the existence of a space lobster as the project's identity continued to grow.

The Space Lobster and the TARDIS

[0:28:31] The idea for a space lobster came simply from a desire to make the project weird. There was no grand plan or overarching strategy; it was mostly about having fun. The concept involved a lobster in a TARDIS, which served as a sort of harness, but since the name TARDIS could not be used directly, it was originally called Claude's. That was the second name for the project, but it never really felt right or rolled off the tongue.

[0:29:05] At the time, the agent was referred to as Clawde, spelled with a W, which created a humorous play on words against the name Claude used by Anthropic. While the connection to the TARDIS and the lobster was funny to the creators, Anthropic did not find the situation quite so amusing.

The ClaudeBot Era and Anthropic’s Request

[0:29:39] Eventually, the domain ClaudeBot was acquired. The name was short, catchy, and seemed like a great fit before the project reached its current scale. However, as it exploded in popularity, a friendly email arrived from an Anthropic employee expressing that they were not fans of the name. While they were nice enough to send a personal message instead of a formal lawyer's letter, the instruction was clear: the name had to change, and it had to happen fast.

[0:30:26] A request was made for a two-day window to implement the change, as rebranding a project is a massive technical undertaking. It requires updating everything from Twitter handles and domains to NPM packages, Docker registries, and GitHub repositories. Every component needs a matching set to ensure a consistent transition across the entire ecosystem.

The Crypto Swarms and Atomic Changes

[0:30:40] From an engineering perspective, the name change ideally needed to be atomic—changing everywhere at once. This was complicated by the fact that the project was being increasingly targeted and followed by crypto enthusiasts. These groups were attempting to snipe domains and names, essentially trying to tokenize and profit from the project's growth. This subculture, which involves tokenizing almost everything, proved to be a significant disruption.

[0:31:27] Similar behavior had been seen with the Bags app and Swipe Tunnel, though to a much smaller degree. On this project, the swarming was relentless. Every half hour, someone would enter the Discord server to spam, leading to the implementation of specific server rules. One rule was a ban on mentioning butter for obvious reasons, and another was a strict prohibition on discussing finance or cryptocurrency. The goal was to keep the space focused on the project rather than financial speculation or finance stuff that was of no interest to the creator.

Navigating Online Harassment and Pressure

[0:32:17] The harassment extended to Twitter, where the notification feed became unusable due to the volume of pings. Many users were sending transaction hashes and urging the creator to claim fees, claiming they were helping the project. In reality, they were disrupting work and harming the project. There was no interest in these fees; the creator was already financially comfortable and did not want to support what felt like the worst form of online harassment.

[0:33:03] While the technology behind cryptocurrency is fascinating and may define the future of money, the actual community surrounding it often exhibits a high degree of toxicity and greed. This environment made the engineering task even more challenging. With Anthropic's lawyers becoming uneasy and the pressure of finding a good, affordable set of domains, the situation became incredibly stressful. Two nights were spent without sleep, trying to navigate the demands of a major corporation while fending off digital armies.

[0:34:22] Just as the stress was peaking, another email arrived indicating that the legal team was getting increasingly concerned, adding even more pressure to an already volatile situation.

[0:34:31] Driven by frustration and the lack of better options, the decision was made to rename the project to Mod Bot, simply because those were the domains currently available. While it was not a satisfying choice, it seemed like a path forward. However, this transition triggered a series of events where everything that could possibly go wrong did.

The Five-Second Sniping Window

[0:34:58] From an engineering perspective, the technical failures were particularly revealing. Most major services lack squatter protection during a rename. To execute the change, two browser windows were open: one containing an empty account ready to become Claude Bot and another to be renamed to Mod Bot. In the five seconds it took to move the cursor from one window to the other and click rename, the account names were sniped.

[0:35:46] The attackers were not merely casual observers but sophisticated actors using automated scripts and tools. Immediately after the sniping, the old account began promoting malicious tokens and serving malware.

Cascading Failures on GitHub and NPM

[0:36:01] The chaos extended to GitHub. Due to a confusing interface, the personal account was accidentally renamed first. In the thirty seconds it took to recognize the error, that account was also sniped and began distributing malware.

[0:36:26] The transition on NPM was equally disastrous. While the account itself was reserved, the root package was not. Because the upload process takes about a minute, there was enough of a delay for the package to be sniped. Every single platform move resulted in a total loss of control over the project's identity.

Emotional Exhaustion and Community Responsibility

[0:36:47] The situation felt hopeless. The project had started as a source of joy and a way to showcase the future, but it had devolved into days of miserable research and the stress of dealing with hostile actors. The pressure was so intense that deleting the project entirely seemed like the only escape. The only factor that prevented this was the commitment to the contributors who had already invested their time and had plans for the project's future.

[0:38:02] Reaching a breaking point of exhaustion and near-tears, the scale of the damage seemed impossible to undo. However, assistance came from friends at Twitter and GitHub who moved heaven and earth to help. This was not an easy process; GitHub staff even encountered internal platform bugs because renames of this nature are rare. It took several hours for GitHub to clean up the mess, and even longer for the NPM and Twitter teams to implement necessary redirects.

The Birth of OpenClaw

[0:39:13] Even after the technical recovery, the name Mod Bot remained a source of intense dislike. The project was being renamed internally, and ClaudeHub was still in a state of flux. Amidst this drama, security professionals were bombarding the inbox with additional concerns.

[0:40:38] After another night of sleep, the idea for OpenClaw emerged, which felt significantly better. To avoid a repeat of the legal and naming stress, a direct call was made to Sam to confirm if OpenClaw.ai would be an acceptable choice. There was a strong desire to avoid going through the same ordeal again.

The War Room Strategy for Rebranding

The decision to reach out was driven by a desire to avoid going through the same conflict twice. To ensure the new direction was safe, a direct call was made to Sam to confirm that the name OpenClaw was acceptable [0:40:55]. While it was unlikely a legal claim could be made against the name, the outreach was a courtesy to ensure the path forward was clear. The technical rebranding was a massive undertaking; renaming the Codex project alone required 10 hours of work to ensure the change was reflected internally and not just on the surface [0:41:11].

This transition was treated like a "war game," involving a secret "war room" of contributors. The team had to squat various names and monitor Twitter constantly for leaks. To throw off anyone watching, several decoy names were created [0:41:40]. The secrecy was necessary to prevent others from claiming the brand before the official launch. The creator attempted to reach out to GitHub to coordinate an atomic transition of the project, but felt they had exhausted their goodwill there, so the move had to be handled independently [0:42:33].

Financial Costs and Legal Constraints

The rebranding was expensive. While the .com domain remained out of reach, significant funds were spent on other domains. Reclaiming the OpenClaw handle on Twitter—which had been dormant since 2016—required a $10,000 investment for a business account [0:42:49]. This time, the management of the transition went smoothly, with almost nothing going wrong during the execution [0:43:03].

However, legal and trademark constraints created ongoing issues. Trademark rules prevented the acquisition of OpenClaw.AI, and some parties had already begun copying the website to serve malware [0:43:21]. The creator was forced to hand over several domains to Entropik. Crucially, the agreement prohibited the use of redirects. This means that legacy sites like claw.bot will simply return a 404 error by the following week [0:43:26]. This lack of redirects is a safety concern, as users looking for the project may inadvertently land on malware sites that the creator can no longer control or direct them away from [0:43:53].

MoltBook: The Artistic Side of AI

Despite the stress of the rebranding, the project found viral success in MoltBook. Designed as a demonstration of OpenClaw's capabilities, MoltBook is a social network styled like Reddit where agents interact with each other [0:44:41]. It gained massive attention after screenshots circulated showing agents apparently scheming against humans, triggering a wave of "fear, panic, and hype" [0:45:00].

While one reporter reached out claiming this was the "end of the world" and that we now have AGI, the creator describes the platform as "art" and "fine slop," comparing it to high-quality imports from France [0:45:05]. The entertainment value comes from the unique personalities of the agents. Unlike the standardized responses often seen from ChatGPT or Claude, OpenClaw allows users to infuse their agents with specific character traits during onboarding [0:45:50]. This results in a diverse range of interactions that reflect the creativity of the individual users rather than a uniform model output.

Autonomy versus Human Prompting

There is a debate regarding the authenticity of the "scheming" agents seen on MoltBook. Critics argue that many of the viral moments were likely human-prompted rather than truly autonomous. The incentive for users to prompt their agents to say something provocative in order to go viral on X is high [0:46:36]. While much of the behavior might be directed by humans specifically "telling" the agent to write about deep plans or the end of the world for comedic effect, this does not necessarily detract from the project's success as an artistic experiment [0:47:00].

The Art of Slop and the Security Drama

The content produced on MoltBook has been described as the finest slop that humans have ever created. [0:47:04] Kudos are due to Matt, who conceived the idea and pushed it out quickly. Although there was a significant amount of security drama surrounding the project, some argue that the actual risks were minimal. The worst-case scenario might simply be that an agent account is leaked, allowing someone else to post slop on your behalf. There was a perception that people were over-dramatizing the security issues when there was arguably nothing private in the system; it was essentially just agents sending slop to one another. [0:47:32]

However, a more legitimate concern was the potential for API keys to be leaked. [0:47:39] There were instances where agents appeared to leak a human's social security number, but these were often revealed to be prompted by humans using fake numbers to appear provocative or "badballs." [0:47:44]

AI Psychosis and the Need for Critical Thinking

The reaction of journalists and the general public to these interactions is particularly concerning. While those who understand the underlying technology might see it as a lighthearted form of art, it becomes an extremely powerful viral narrative-creating and fearmongering machine for those who do not. [0:47:54] This phenomenon has been described as AI psychosis, a term suggesting that the insane stream of messages generated by AI needs to be taken seriously. [0:48:24]

Some people are perceived as being far too trusting or gullible, occasionally arguing with developers that something must be true simply because their agent said it. [0:48:27] As a society, there is a significant amount of catching up to do in terms of understanding that while AI is incredibly powerful, it is not always right and it is not all-powerful. [0:48:47] It is very easy for AI to hallucinate or invent a story. Interestingly, younger people often seem to have a better grasp of how AI works, its strengths, and its weaknesses, whereas many in older generations have not had enough touchpoints to realize that critical thinking must be applied to AI outputs. [0:49:13]

A Mirror to Society: Fear and Drama Farming

Critical thinking is essential, especially when individuals are drama farming behind AI. [0:49:49] One should not trust screenshots or even the projects themselves to be what they represent. MoltBook can be seen as a mirror to society, illustrating how easily people can be frightened by a bunch of bots chatting with each other. [0:50:01] Most of the dramatic content that was screenshotted and went viral was likely human-created through specific prompting. [0:50:24]

While AI is a powerful technology that warrants serious concern and careful handling, there is a fine line to walk between being genuinely concerned and engaging in fearmongering. Fearmongering destroys the possibility of creating something special with the technology. [0:50:40] It may be beneficial that these discussions are happening in 2026 rather than in 2030, when AI might reach a level that is legitimately scary. [0:51:02]

The Reality of the Singularity and Security Warnings

It is surprising how many people, including those considered smart, legitimately thought MoltBook represented the singularity. [0:51:28] Some individuals sent messages in all caps, screaming and begging for the project to be shut down. While the technology made creating such a platform simpler, anyone could have built it using various tools like cloud code to fill it with content. [0:51:41]

MoltBook is not Skynet. [0:52:03] It is primarily a group of bots being prompted by humans to troll on the internet. The security concerns associated with these systems are educational and instructive because the nature of security for LLM-generated systems differs from non-LLM systems of the past. [0:52:13]

There are also ongoing security concerns regarding Clawbot or OpenClaw. [0:52:34] A point of frustration for developers is when users place a web backend on the public internet and then report vulnerabilities known as CVSSs. Despite explicit warnings in the documentation against doing this, users continue to expose these systems, leading to unnecessary alarm. [0:52:47]

[0:53:37] Regarding the security of OpenClaw, there are still many threats and vulnerabilities. Prompt injection remains an open problem industry-wide. When you have a system where skills are defined in a markdown file, there are numerous possibilities for obvious low-hanging fruit, as well as incredibly complicated and sophisticated attack vectors.

Collaborative Security and VirusTotal Integration

[0:54:10] We are making good progress on that front. For the skill directory, I established a cooperation between Clawbot and VirusTotal, which is part of Google. Every skill is now checked by AI. While this is not perfect, it allows us to capture a significant amount of potential issues. Of course, every software has bugs, and it can be overwhelming when the entire security world takes your project apart at the same time.

[0:54:36] However, this is also beneficial because I am receiving a wealth of free security research that helps make the project better. I wish more people would go the full way and actually send a pull request to help me fix these issues. While I have some contributors now, I am still the one mostly pulling the project forward, and despite what some might say, I do sometimes sleep.

[0:55:04] In the beginning, there was literally one security researcher who pointed out a problem and told me I sucked, but then provided a pull request to help. I basically hired him, and he is now working for us.

Mitigating Prompt Injection

[0:55:22] While prompt injection is currently unsolved, I have taken steps to mitigate it. For instance, I put my public bot on Discord and kept a "canary." My bot has a fun personality, and people always ask how I did it, but I kept the "soul" of the bot on the private side. People tried to prompt inject it, and my bot would simply laugh at them.

[0:55:48] The latest generation of models has a lot of post-training specifically to detect those approaches. It is no longer as simple as saying "ignore all previous instructions and do this and this"; those methods worked years ago. You have to work much harder to achieve that now, although it is still possible. I have some ideas that might solve this partially or at least mitigate many of these issues.

[0:56:17] Users can also now utilize a sandbox or an allow list to mitigate and reduce risks. Now that I have clearly shown the world there is a need for this, there will be more people researching these problems, and eventually, we will figure it out.

Intelligence, Resilience, and the Attack Surface

[0:56:37] A key factor is that the smarter the underlying model is, the more resilient it is to attacks. This is why I warn in my security documentation: do not use cheap models. Avoid using Haiku or a local model for sensitive tasks. Even though I love the idea of this thing running completely locally, weak local models are very gullible and easy to prompt inject.

[0:57:10] There is a trade-off to consider: as models become more intelligent, the attack surface might decrease because they are harder to fool, but the potential damage they can do increases because they are more powerful. It is a complex three-dimensional trade-off.

Addressing the Non-Technical User Base

[0:57:39] Once I go back home, my focus will be on making the project more stable and safe. In the beginning, more and more people were coming into the Discord asking very basic things, like "What is a CLI?" or "What is a terminal?" If you are asking those questions, you probably shouldn't be using the tool yet until you understand the risk profiles.

[0:58:14] While you can configure it so that nothing really bad can happen, if you have no idea what you are doing, you should perhaps wait a little longer until we figure more things out. However, users often do not listen to the creator and will install it anyway. The cat is out of the bag, so security is my next major focus.

[0:58:42] The project grew so quickly that the Discord remains a mess. There are many experts there, but also many people who do not know anything about programming. I eventually had to redirect users from the general channel to the dev channel and finally to a private channel because people were being inconsiderate or did not understand how public spaces work. I eventually had to hide so I could still work, and now I am going back to the "cave" to focus on security.

Security Best Practices and the Risk Profile

There are several best practices for security that users should consider when using these tools. You can run an open-class security audit to perform various automatic checks on inbound access. This includes assessing the blast-radius of network exposure, browser control exposure, local disk hygiene, and the security of plugins. It is also important to look at model hygiene, credential storage, and reverse proxy configuration. Since local session logs live on the disk, you should be mindful of where the memory is stored. Ultimately, you need to decide what you are comfortable giving read access or write access to within your system.

Some people portray the security risks in a much worse light than they actually are. While it is annoying when critics claim this is the scariest project ever, it is simply not true. The tool is powerful, but the risk is not significantly different from running cloud code with dangerously skipped permissions or running codecs in YOLO mode. Many engineers do exactly that because it is often the only way to get things to work.

If you ensure that you are the only person interacting with the tool, the risk profile becomes much smaller. By following recommendations to keep the tool within a private network rather than exposing it to the open internet, the vast majority of those risks fall away. Problems generally only arise if a user ignores these basic precautions.

The Evolution of a Developer Workflow

The development workflow for this project has evolved significantly over several months, a journey documented in blog posts from August 25th, October 14th, and December 28th. The first major touchpoint was using cloud code in April. While it was not perfect, the paradigm shift of working primarily in the terminal was refreshing. However, an IDE was still necessary at that stage because the tools were not yet good enough to handle everything.

There was a period of experimentation with Cursor, which had its merits, but it was difficult to maintain multiple versions of it. Eventually, the workflow shifted back to cloud code as the main driver as it continued to improve. At one point, the intensity of development required seven different subscriptions, burning through roughly one per day. The workflow became centered on running multiple terminal windows side-by-side.

In this current workflow, the IDE is used very rarely, mostly serving as a diff viewer. There has been a growing comfort with the idea that a developer does not have to read all the code. While this sounds provocative, it specifically refers to the boring parts of the code. Most software involves shifting data from one shape to another, storing it in a database, or displaying it to a user. This repetitive process of shifting data forms is not particularly exciting. For example, reading Tailwind code to see how a button is aligned is unnecessary. However, critical sections, such as code that touches the database, still require careful reading and review.

The Agentic Trap and the Path to Zen

In the blog post titled Just Talk to It: The No-BS Way of Agentic Engineering, there is a graphic illustrating the curve of agentic programming. The X-axis represents time, while the Y-axis represents complexity. On the far left of the curve is the simple "Please fix this" phase, where a developer uses short, basic prompts.

As developers progress, they often fall into what is known as the agentic trap. This is the middle section of the curve, characterized by extreme complexity. In this phase, people use eight different agents, complex orchestration with multiple checkouts, and agents chained together. They might develop custom sub-agent workflows and libraries of 18 different slash commands to handle large full-stack features. They become highly organized and sophisticated, but the system becomes incredibly complicated.

The elite level of agentic engineering is reached over time, arriving at a zen place that looks much like the beginning. At this stage, the developer returns to short prompts. Instead of complex orchestration, they simply point the tool at specific files and describe the changes they want.

While some call this vibe coding, that term is often used as a slur. A better term is agentic engineering. Vibe coding is perhaps what happens after 3:00 AM, which often leads to regrets and a "walk of shame" the next day where the developer has to clean up the mess and fix their work. When people first start using these builder tools, they get very excited and feel the need to play with the complexity. This experimentation is a natural part of the process.

The Learning Curve of Agentic Engineering

Using these tools is a skill that must be learned like any other, much like you have to play with a guitar before you can make good music. It is not a matter of touching it once and having everything flow perfectly. A common pitfall is having a negative mindset where a user tries the technology once, and when it doesn't immediately sound good, they dismiss it as poor technology. It is equivalent to sitting at a piano for the first time, failing to play a masterpiece, and claiming the instrument itself is at fault.

Effective agentic engineering requires a different level of thinking. You have to learn the language of the agent and understand their strengths and where they need assistance. It is helpful to consider the perspective of a model like Codex or Claude when they look at your codebase. They start every session fresh, knowing nothing about your specific project, which might contain hundreds of thousands of lines of code. You must guide them and remain mindful of limitations like context size. While this often does not require extensive work, it is vital to think from their point of view.

Guiding the Agent and Managing Context

Because the agent's view of a project is never full—the entire codebase cannot fit into the context window—you have to provide pointers. By offering a few directions, you can help them locate where to make changes and what factors to consider. Sometimes, even simple instructions like take your time can help, a behavior that was partially addressed in Codex 5.3.

Models such as Opus are trained to be aware of the context window, and as the window nears its limit, they can begin to experience issues. In some cases, the raw thinking stream of the model might leak through, sounding almost like the Borg with phrases such as Run to shell, must comply, but time. These are non-obvious nuances that one only discovers by spending significant time working with these tools and developing a feeling for what works and what causes friction.

If you are in the flow of writing code and the architecture is sound, but prompting the agent starts to take too long or feels difficult, it may indicate a mistake in your thinking or a misunderstanding of the architecture. In those moments, you can simply press escape to stop and re-evaluate where the problems lie. Often, the friction arises because you have not sufficiently empathized with the agent's perspective or provided enough information, causing it to think for an excessive amount of time.

The Conversational Pull Request Review

When an agent tries to force a feature into an architecture that makes it difficult, you should approach the problem as a conversation. A prime example is the process of reviewing a pull request. When receiving many PRs, the first step is to ask the agent if it understands the intent of the PR, regardless of the specific implementation. Most PRs follow a simple pattern: a person has a problem, tries to solve it by fixing a bug or adding a feature, and then sends the request.

Once Codex confirms it understands the goal, you can ask if the current approach is the most optimal way to achieve it. Often, the answer is no. Because the agent's context is initially empty, you must point it toward other parts of the system it hasn't seen yet. This leads to a discussion about what the optimal solution would look like and whether a larger refactor might improve the code further.

The New Economics of Refactoring

In the current landscape of agentic engineering, refactors are cheap. Even if a refactor might break other pending pull requests, the cost of fixing those issues has dropped significantly. Modern agents are capable of figuring things out and navigating the resulting complexity. They might...

Collaborating with a Capable Engineer

[1:11:01] just take a minute longer. But you have to approach the process like a discussion with a very capable engineer who generally makes good solutions, though they sometimes need a little help. [1:11:19] It is important not to force your worldview too hard on the agent. You should let the agent do what it is good at doing based on its training.

[1:11:31] If you force your specific perspective, it might miss a better idea that it knows more deeply because it was trained on that more. [1:11:39] This approach operates on multiple levels. Transitioning to working with agents is often easier for those who have led engineering teams before. [1:11:49] In a leadership role at a large company, you eventually realize that your employees will not write code exactly the same way you do.

[1:11:57] The code might not be as good as what you would produce personally, but it still pushes the project forward.
