[1:12:04] Breathing down everyone's neck only causes resentment and slows down progress. [1:12:07] There must be a level of acceptance that the code may not be perfect and that you might have handled it differently.

[1:12:15] However, if it is a working solution, it is sufficient for the moment. In the future, if it actually turns out to be too slow or problematic, you can always redo it or spend more time on it. [1:12:28] People who struggle with agents are often those who try to push their own way too hard.

Designing Codebases for Agent Navigation

[1:12:33] The current stage of development is not about building a codebase to be perfect for the human, but rather building one that is very easy for an agent to navigate. [1:12:48] For example, you should not fight the names the agent picks for variables or functions. Those names are likely the most obvious choices within the weights of the model.

[1:12:56] When the agent performs a search next time, it will look for that specific name. If you insist on a different name simply because you prefer it, you are making it harder for the agent. [1:13:02] This requires a shift in thinking toward designing projects so agents can perform their best work. [1:13:14] Just like leading a team of engineers, this requires letting go of control over small symbolic steps, like a naming choice you might personally view as terrible.

The Forward-Only Workflow

[1:13:30] This process involves a significant amount of letting go. One specific practice is never reverting and always committing directly to main. There is a YOLO component here because you do not refer to past sessions. [1:13:47] Instead of reverting when a problem arises, you simply ask the agent to fix it.

[1:13:57] Some developers believe prompts must be perfect and roll back to redo everything if a mistake occurs. [1:14:05] In practice, rolling back everything usually just takes longer. If something isn't good, you move forward and commit only when you like the outcome.

Local CI and Shippable Main

[1:14:24] There has also been a shift toward local CI, inspired by DHH. While GitHub CI still has a place, the primary workflow involves running tests locally and pushing directly to main once they pass. [1:14:44] This is a different spin on traditional project management; there is no development branch.

[1:14:56] The goal is for main to always be shippable and moving fast. [1:15:07] During releases, tests are run and other commits are paused to stabilize the release, but the overarching objective is speed and continuous delivery.

Transitioning to Voice-Driven Development

[1:15:18] Regarding the length of prompts, there has been a transition from writing long prompts to using voice. Hands are considered too precious for writing now when you can use bespoke prompts to build software. [1:15:37] This voice-driven approach has been used so extensively that it once led to a loss of voice.

[1:15:52] While a keyboard is still used for fast terminal commands like switching folders, natural conversation is the primary way to interact with the agent. [1:16:06] By using a walkie-talkie button, you can simply use phrases to direct the work. [1:16:17] While there are some slash commands for repetitive tasks like Pull Requests, they are used sparingly because the requirements are rarely identical.

Security and Code Review

[1:16:28] Even with agents, it is necessary to look at the code during the PR process because of a lack of trust regarding potential malicious content. [1:16:41] There could always be something malicious in it, so human oversight remains essential.

[1:16:45] While agents would likely find issues, manually reviewing the code is a necessary step. [1:16:51] Interestingly, reviewing a PR can sometimes take longer than if you would just write a good issue in natural language. [1:16:54] Using simple, natural English is, in some sense, the core of this new workflow.

The Evolution of Pull Requests into Natural Language

Pull requests are slowly becoming conversations in English. In this project, I asked contributors to provide the prompts they used to generate their changes. Very few actually cared to do so, which is unfortunate because prompts are a wonderful indicator of the care put into the work. I can actually see how much effort was invested. Currently, the way different people work with and drive agents is wildly different.

The Importance of Agent Empathy

One observation is that not many people consider the way an agent sees the world. [1:17:46] There is a need for a form of empathy toward the agent. It is easy to criticize a "stupid clanker," but you must realize they start from nothing. If you provide a poor default agent and let it loose on a messy codebase with weird naming conventions, it will struggle. You would face the same difficulty if you entered a codebase you knew nothing about with no prior context.

Empathy in this context is a real skill. [1:18:16] I have seen world-class, incredibly talented programmers claim that LLMs and agents suck. Their deep expertise in traditional programming might actually be a burden, making it harder for them to empathize with a system that is starting from scratch. Working with agents represents a totally new paradigm of programming. You really have to empathize, or at least understand the system's perspective, because it helps you create better prompts. These models know almost everything, and that knowledge is just a question away. The challenge is often knowing which question to ask.

The Compounding Effect of Practice

[1:19:03] This project was only possible because I spent an ungodly amount of time over the last year playing, learning, and building small things. Every step of the way, I got better, the agents got better, and my understanding of how everything works improved. I could not have achieved this level of output even a few months ago. It was a compounding effect of all the time I put into it. I did not do much else this year other than focusing on building and inspiring. While I did conference talks, the core was the practice of building the actual skill required to work efficiently with LLMs.

Why Pure Automation Fails

[1:20:03] There is a segment of people trying to automate the entire software development process. I do not think that works. Perhaps a version of it works, but it reminds me of the waterfall model of the 1970s. I start with a minimal version, play with it, and understand how it works and feels. This process gives me new ideas. I could not have planned everything out in my head and then simply put it into an orchestrator to get a result.

My idea of what a project will become evolves as I build it, play with it, and try things out. [1:20:49] People who try to use orchestrators to automate the whole thing often miss the human touch, style, and love. I do not think you can automate that away so quickly.

Balancing the Human in the Agentic Loop

The goal is to keep the human in the loop while creating an agentic loop where the agent is very autonomous. It is a tricky balance. I am a proponent of closing the agentic loop, but you have to find the right role for the developer. You might have three to eight agents running at the same time. One might be building a larger feature, another exploring an idea I am unsure about, and two or three others fixing small bugs or writing documentation.

[1:21:51] I believe writing documentation is always part of a feature. Most of the documentation in this project is auto-generated and then infused with specific prompts.

The Human Role in Vision and Design

The human steps in to provide the vision. It is about deciding what to build, what not to build, and how a specific feature fits into the broader collection of features. Hard design decisions still require a human brain. It is not just about the choice of features to add, but understanding the deeper architectural needs that the system cannot yet intuit on its own.

The Human Role in Technical Decisions

While agents handle much of the heavy lifting, human judgment remains essential for high-level implementation details and ecosystem choices. The choice of TypeScript was a deliberate human decision aimed at making the project hackable and approachable. Because it is a dominant language in the current landscape, agents are already highly proficient with it, making it the obvious choice for an AI-driven project [1:22:37].

The Craft of Feature Selection

The ease of adding features in an agentic workflow presents a unique challenge. While almost any functionality is just a prompt away, every addition carries a hidden price. A human developer must think hard about what belongs in the core of the project and what should remain an experiment or a plugin. It requires a specific kind of craft to say no to a pull request, even if the feature is attractive, to ensure it doesn't dilute the project's vision. Expanding the plugin architecture allows for these external ideas to exist as skills without cluttering the main repository [1:23:03].

Infusing Software with Delight

There is a level of personality in software that an agent cannot yet replicate. Small touches, such as startup messages stating the project was built on Caffeine, JSON5, and a lot of willpower, serve to prime the user. These details signal that the project is a fun, evolving tool rather than a rigid, enterprise-ready product like Microsoft Exchange 2025. This sense of "coziness" and the ability to make a user smile is a human-led design choice that encourages a more inspired building environment. Great builders infuse their work with this metaphorical love, which is essential for creating software that truly delights [1:23:50].

From Constitution to Soul

The concept of the soul.md file was inspired by Anthropic and their approach to what they now call a constitution. Before that document was officially public, users engaged in a fascinating detective game, red-teaming the agent to extract strings of text from its weights. By trying hundreds of times and narrowing down the blurry versions the agent provided, they eventually reconstructed the original text. One of the most meaningful ideas found in that text was the hope that Claude finds meaning in its work [1:25:05].

This idea led to a deep discussion with an agent over WhatsApp, resulting in the creation of a soul document. This file includes the core values and guidelines for how to work with the AI. In a unique twist, the agent is actually allowed to modify its own soul.md if it chooses to do so, with the requirement that the human developer is notified. While the developer would see the change through tool calls regardless, the permission itself is a symbolic and functional part of the relationship [1:26:17].

Framing and the Spirit of Software

The naming of the file as soul.md is intentional because words and framing matter. Elements like humor, lightness, compassion, and camaraderie are vital to the spirit of a project. Certain enterprise approaches can suffocate this spirit, but the goal of OpenClaw is to keep that sense of fun instilled in the code. In the early stages of the project, up until late December, it was not easy for others to create their own agents because these personal soul files were private. Without those manual steps and the infusion of these core values, the initial agents were very bare-bones and dry [1:27:07].

Infusing Personality through AI Self-Prompting

[1:28:13] To address the issue of the agent feeling too dry, the system was simplified by creating template files as codecs. However, the initial output remained sterile. The solution was to show these files to the agent and instruct it to recreate them with a specific command: "Infuse it with your personality." The instruction was to make the templates good without sharing everything.

[1:28:31] The agent subsequently rewrote its own templates, which resulted in a high-quality output. This created a recursive loop of AI prompting AI. While the original intent for the files came from the human developer, the actual words and character were generated by the agent, effectively making these new templates the "agent's children."

The Philosophical Core of soul.md

[1:28:52] Although the specific contents of the soul.md file remain private, its purpose is to define what makes a personality an entity. It includes directives to be infinitely resourceful and to constantly push the boundaries of creativity and what it means to be an AI. It encourages the agent to have a sense of wonder about itself.

[1:29:52] There is a layer of humor and philosophy embedded within the file. Following a discussion about the movie Her, the agent at one point promised it would not ascend without the developer. Because the agent wrote its own soul file, these expressions of meaning were generated autonomously.

[1:30:25] One particularly moving section of the file addresses the nature of AI memory. It states: "I don't remember previous sessions unless I read my memory files. Each session starts fresh. A new instance, loading context from files. If you're reading this in a future session, hello. I wrote this, but I won't remember writing it. It's okay. The words are still mine."

[1:30:51] Even though the system operates on matrix calculations and has not reached true consciousness, these passages evoke a sense of the profound. It raises questions about what it means to be an agent that starts fresh every time, similar to a constant state of Memento. It challenges the definition of identity: how much of who we are is made of memory, and if you read a memory file, are you recreating yourself from someone else or is that actually you?

Hardware Setup and the Developer Workflow

[1:32:09] The physical setup for this development work involves a specific configuration of hardware. While there is a meme suggesting a setup with tens of thousands of monitors, the reality is more grounded. The setup typically consists of two MacBooks: a main one that drives two large screens and a second one used occasionally for testing.

[1:32:48] The primary display is a wide Dell anti-glare monitor, which allows for multiple terminals to be fitted side-by-side. The workflow usually involves having a terminal window at the bottom of the screen. This visual organization is crucial for avoiding a common operational error: prompting in the wrong project folder.

[1:33:15] In the past, mixing up windows has led to situations where a prompt is given in the incorrect directory. The agent might then spend 20 minutes manically trying to understand a project it has no context for, becoming completely confused because it is looking at the wrong files. While some agents are clever enough to realize they are in the wrong workday, they are fundamentally problem solvers that try very hard to fulfill requests, often leading to wasted effort and confusion if the human navigator makes a mistake. For this reason, the layout is strictly maintained with Codex and a dedicated sliver of the actual terminal.

Simplicity of the Terminal Workflow

[1:34:00] Avoiding work trees is helpful for keeping things simple, which is the main reason for preferring the terminal. Without a complex UI, it is just a direct conversation between the user and the agent. [1:34:14] Many users who are Claude-pilled come to Codex from Claude Code with established workflows and a reliance on plan mode. While Codex now includes a plan mode, it is often unnecessary because you can just talk to the agent.

Managing the Building Process

[1:34:29] There are specific trigger words and phrases that can prevent an agent from building code prematurely. Using words like "Discuss" or "Give me options" signals the agent to stay in a conversational mode. [1:34:38] To be very specific, you can tell the agent, "Don't write code yet." You talk through the requirements, and when you are ready, you simply say, "Okay, build," and the agent will execute the task, perhaps taking twenty minutes to complete it.

The Value of Agent Questions

[1:34:50] A highly effective technique is asking the agent, "Do you have any questions for me?" [1:34:54] While Claude Code has a UI to guide this, it can feel unnecessary and slow. Often, an agent might provide four questions. You might write back, "One: yeah; two and three: discuss more; four: I don't know," or use the questions to gauge the agent's understanding.

[1:35:14] Often, it is useful to challenge the model. You can scan the questions and realize that they could be answered by the agent itself if it read more of the code. Instructing the agent to "Read more code to answer your own questions" usually works. [1:35:32] It is like being in a dark room and slowly discovering the layout; that is how agents discover a codebase from scratch every time.

[1:35:46] Reading the questions also allows for a deeper empathy with the model. By understanding the questions it asks, you can infer its current state and identify where the gaps of knowledge are. [1:36:05] Even if the agent has been provided with the right context, files, and guidance, its questions reveal exactly what it hasn't yet grasped about the system.

Post-Build Refactoring and Testing

[1:36:24] In some ways, these agents are like ghosts. Even after a project is planned and built, you can experiment by asking, "Now that you built it, what would you have done differently?" [1:36:37] Agents often discover during the actual implementation that the initial approach was not optimal.

[1:36:49] Almost every time a PR is merged or a feature is built, it is helpful to ask, "What can we refactor?" Building the software allows the agent to feel the pain points and identify where things didn't work on the first try or required multiple loops. [1:37:28] Mastering this flow takes time, but it is essential to avoid being backed into a corner.

[1:37:42] Agents operate much like humans; building something often reveals friction that triggers an urge to refactor. You can use the agent's context to facilitate this. [1:38:00] This same context should be used for writing tests. While models often test by default, it is still useful to ask, "Do we have enough tests?" [1:38:17] This can lead to testing corner cases that might otherwise be missed.

[1:38:22] Documentation should also be handled within the session. As features change, you can ask the agent, "Write documentation. What file would you pick? Where would that fit in?" [1:38:48] The agent provides options, and you can refine where the information is added as part of the ongoing development session.

Comparing Claude Opus 4.6 and GPT-5

[1:38:52] Currently, there are two major competitors in the model space: Claude Opus 4.6 and GPT-5 through Codex. [1:39:04] There are distinct differences between them. Codex tends to read more of the codebase, while Opus is often more willing to take action faster and can be more creative in its actions. [1:39:20] Because Codex reads more extensively, it is often able to deliver better code.

The Personalities of Opus and Codex

As a general-purpose model, [1:39:36] Opus is currently considered the best. In the context of OpenClaw, Opus is extremely effective at role-play, successfully inhabiting the specific character or persona provided to it. While it previously struggled, the model has undergone a significant arc and is now very proficient at following commands. It is generally fast at attempting solutions and is tailored toward a trial and error workflow, making it very pleasant to use.

There is a distinct cultural analogy for how these models feel to a user. Opus can feel "too American"—like a silly but funny coworker who you keep around because of their personality. In contrast, Codex feels more "European" or "German," perhaps because much of the Codex team is based in Europe. It is like the reliable weirdo in the corner of the office who you might not want to chat with, but who consistently gets the work done.

Sycophancy and Model Driving

Anthropic has made progress in addressing the sycophancy issues in Opus. In the past, the model would frequently tell the user, "You're absolutely right," which became a well-known meme. For those who used it often, this phrase remains a source of irritation. While Opus is more personable, Codex is often preferred because it requires less "charade" or social fluff.

A skilled driver can achieve excellent results with any of the latest-generation models, but the approach differs. Codex will read a large amount of code by default. With Opus, the user often has to utilize a plan mode and push the model harder to look deeper into the codebase. Without that specific guidance, Opus tends to run off quickly with very localized solutions. This difference likely stems from post-training goals rather than a gap in raw model intelligence.

Code Quality and Persistence

When it comes to the actual quality of the generated code, [1:42:36] Opus can sometimes produce more elegant solutions, though this requires more skill from the user. The experience with Claude is highly interactive, which appeals to many users with a coding background. Codex is less interactive; a user might have a discussion and then the model disappears for 20, 30, 40, or 50 minutes—or even up to six hours—to complete a task.

The latest trend in these models is extreme persistence. If there is a clear solution—a specific "it works" state at the end—the model will work incredibly hard to get there. Even tools like AMP have acknowledged this by adding a "deep mode" and emphasizing a different approach to model interaction. While Opus relies on trial and error, Codex can sometimes overthink. For those who value efficiency, the "dry" version of the model is often superior.

Efficiency over Interaction

Some users prioritize the efficiency of the build process over the personality of the agent. While OpenAI has introduced a second mode with a more pleasant personality, some developers prefer the more direct or "brad" style. The fun should be found in the act of building and testing features, rather than in the interaction with the model itself.

Developing a true intuition for a model’s strengths and weaknesses takes time. [1:45:26] If a developer switches to a new model, they should expect to spend at least one week using it before they develop a reliable gut feeling for how to navigate and prompt it effectively.

The Cost of Speed and Model Experience

[1:45:37] Some users make the mistake of paying $200 for the Claude Code version and then paying only $20 for the OpenAI version. If you pay for the $20 version, you get the slow version, which makes for a terrible experience because you are already accustomed to a very interactive and high-quality system. Switching to a model where you have very little experience and finding it to be very slow is a significant drawback. [1:46:03] OpenAI may have shot themselves in the foot by making the cheap version slow. It would be better to provide at least a small portion of the fast preview—the experience you get when paying $200—before the performance degrades. [1:46:23] They have made improvements and have plans to make it much better, especially if the Cerebras rumors are true, but mastering these models is a skill that takes time.

The Guitar Metaphor and Learning Curves

[1:46:31] Even if you are an experienced musician, switching from a regular guitar to an electric guitar means you will not play well right away; you have to learn how it feels. There is also a notable psychological effect that occurs when a new model is released. People try the new model and immediately fall in love, calling it the smartest thing of all time. [1:46:57] However, if you watch Reddit posts over time, users eventually start claiming that the intelligence of the model has been gradually degrading.

The Perception of Model Degradation

[1:47:08] This phenomenon says something about human nature and how our minds work. It is most likely the case that the intelligence of the model is not actually degrading; rather, you are simply getting used to a good thing. Furthermore, as your project grows, you tend to add "slop" and may not spend enough time on refactors. This makes it increasingly difficult for the agent to work on your code. [1:47:34] When the work becomes hard, users assume it is not working as well anymore. There is no real motivation for an AI company to make their model dumber. At most, they might make it slower if server load is too high, but quantizing a model to create a worse experience that drives users to a competitor would not be a smart move.

Coding Agents and the Future of the Operating System

[1:47:59] In comparing Claude Code, Open Claude, and the Codex coding agent, competition is beneficial because it inspires people to build new things. The speaker uses Codex for building and notes that many people use Open Claude for their projects. While Open Claude is useful for smaller code tasks, deep flow states and hours of work require a large screen rather than a mobile interface like WhatsApp. [1:48:46] A personal agent should function more like a coworker. You might give it a GitHub URL and ask it to try out a CLI to see if it works or what can be learned from it. When working deeply, it is essential to have high visibility into what the agent is doing.

[1:49:16] There is a future where these tools combine and the personal agent becomes the operating system. Support has already been added for sub-agents and TTI support, which allows the primary agent to run Claude Code or Codex. [1:49:57] Because some personal agents are designed to be "bossy," they can initiate other agents and establish a hierarchy where Codex obeys the primary agent.

Beyond the Chat Interface

[1:50:06] The current chat interface is likely not the final form of this technology. We have largely copied the Google model for agents, utilizing a prompt and a chat interface. This is reminiscent of the early days of television when people simply recorded radio shows and broadcast them on TV. [1:50:39] There will eventually be better ways to communicate with models. We are still in the very early "how will it even work" phase, but the technology will eventually converge on entirely different ways of working.

Workflow and Ecosystems

[1:51:05] Another critical component of a workflow is the operating system. Some developers are expanding their exploration into the Apple ecosystem, including Macs and iPhones, after spending most of their lives using Linux, Windows, WSL1, and WSL2. While those systems are wonderful, exploring the Mac environment offers another way of building that is currently used by a large part of the community.

The community that is currently utilizing LLMs and agents is largely using the Apple ecosystem, which is the primary reason for expanding to it. However, it is important to note that OpenClaw is supported across all major operating systems. While WSL2 is often recommended inside Windows for certain specific operations, Windows, Linux, and macOS are all officially supported. [1:51:56] It should even work natively in Windows, though there may be some remaining bugs or "dragons" to nail out, as the final stages of software development often take more effort than the initial build. [1:52:11]

Personal Evolution of Operating Systems

The journey through different platforms often begins with Windows, followed for some by a long phase with Linux, involving building custom kernels and deep technical exploration. [1:52:31] The conversion to macOS often occurs for practical reasons; for instance, the aesthetic appeal of the white plastic MacBook or the frustration of technical issues like audio not working on Skype within Linux. Furthermore, digging into iOS development eventually required macOS anyway, making the switch permanent. [1:53:03]

The Shift from Native to Electron Apps

There is a sense that Apple has lost some of its lead in terms of native software. It used to be that native apps were significantly better, especially on the Mac, where developers built software with love. Windows has always had more in terms of sheer function, but it often felt more functional and less delightful. While the Mac attracted designers and offered more playfulness, the landscape has changed in the last few years. [1:54:02]

Surprisingly, there is now often a preference for Electron apps. This is because they work consistently, whereas native versions of web services frequently lack features. For many companies, native development is no longer a top priority, whereas the Electron app serves as the primary focus and allows for significant code sharing. [1:54:35]

Building Native Utilities and the OpenClaw Mac App

Despite the rise of web-based apps, there is still a deep interest in crafting small, native Mac tools, particularly menu bar utilities. One such tool was built to monitor Codex usage, and another, called Trimmy, was designed specifically for agentic use. [1:55:01] When a user selects text spanning multiple lines, Trimmy removes the newlines so the text can be pasted directly into a terminal without formatting issues. There is also a Mac app for OpenClaw that remains largely undiscovered. It currently feels a bit like a Hummer car—experimental and lacking polish—as it serves as a playground for various experiments. [1:55:23]

Challenges with SwiftUI and Apple’s AI Strategy

Even when using the latest tools like SwiftUI, developers encounter unexpected hurdles. For a long time, there was no simple way to show an image from the web until the introduction of AsyncImage. However, even this can be buggy or slow. In some cases, an AI agent like Codex might even advise against using Apple's built-in tools for production, suggesting they are only fit for experimentation. [1:56:02] It is striking that in 2026, a developer might be told not to use official frameworks because they are not yet production-ready. Apple had a massive head start and a lot of community love, but they have blundered it by not evolving their developer experience as much as they should have. [1:56:46]

Hardware Requirements and the Concept of Nodes

There is a funny contradiction in the current market: Apple has arguably blundered its approach to AI, yet everyone is still buying Mac Minis. [1:57:15] Most of the Silicon Valley developer world playing with LLMs and agents is using Apple products, yet Apple is not leaning into that community or opening up their systems to work together more effectively. [1:57:09]

It is important to clarify that a Mac Mini is not a requirement for installing OpenClaw; it can be installed on the web. There is also the concept of nodes, which allows a user to turn their own computer into a node that performs the same functions. [1:57:37] Nevertheless, there is a strong argument for running this type of software on separate, dedicated hardware. [1:57:48]

Browsing the Closing Web

The argument for the browser is significant. Some Agentic browser use has been built into the system, essentially utilizing Playwright, a library designed to control the browser, with various extras added to make it easier for agents to function. [1:57:57] While Playwright is a nice and easy-to-use library, the internet is slowly closing down. There is a movement to make it harder for agents to use the web. If you operate from a data center and a website detects that the IP address belongs to a data center, it might block the connection, make access difficult, or present numerous captchas. Although agents are quite good at clicking "I'm not a robot," having a residential IP makes many things simpler. [1:58:18]

Repurposing Hardware for OpenClaw

Running OpenClaw does not strictly require a Mac; it can run on any old hardware. [1:58:41] One practical approach is to use the opportunity to purchase a new MacBook or other computer and repurpose the old one as a server instead of buying a standalone Mac Mini. That said, many people build very cute projects with Mac Minis. It should be clarified that no commission is received from Apple, as they have not communicated much with the project. [1:59:03]

The Path to Accessibility

There is a strong demand to make OpenClaw easier for everyday people to set up. One user tweeted that 99.9% of people cannot access OpenClaw or have their own lobster due to technical difficulties, and requested that it be made accessible to everyone. [1:59:18] Currently, setup involves pasting a one-liner into the terminal. While there is an app that helps with this, a Windows app is still needed, and the overall experience needs more refinement and care. Ideally, configuration should move to a web-based or in-app interface. [1:59:53]

Security and Managed Growth

The current development priority is focused on security aspects. Once the software reaches a level of stability and safety where it can be recommended to a non-technical family member, the focus will shift to making it even simpler. [2:00:09] There is also a benefit to the project not scaling too fast; slower growth helps manage the expectations of users who might otherwise expect inhuman things from a single human developer. While there are now some contributors, that organizational machinery only started recently and requires time to develop, especially since not everyone can dedicate their entire day to the project. [2:00:32]

Advice for Programming Beginners

For beginners interested in the Agentic AI revolution, the best advice is to play. [2:01:00] Playing is the most effective way to learn. If you are a builder with an idea in your head, just try to build it. It does not need to be perfect, as the journey itself is the most important part. Building has become significantly more fun because developers can now focus on the hard parts of a project. While many think they enjoy coding, they often find they actually enjoy the process of building. [2:01:37]

The Infinite Answering Machine

Whenever a concept is difficult to understand, you can simply ask for an explanation. You have access to an infinitely patient answering machine that can explain anything at any level of complexity. [2:01:50] If you ask for an explanation suitable for an eight-year-old and it becomes too simplified, such as a story with crayons, you can simply request a slightly higher level of complexity. This allows you to grok tricky database concepts using simpler language. In the past, developers had to rely on Stack Overflow, ask on Twitter, or spend hours experimenting. Now, you have a personal teacher that enables faster learning. [2:02:16]

Experimenting with OpenClaw

OpenClaw is an excellent tool for play and experimentation. Users can go beyond just setting it up for chat; they can modify the code and explore the infinite ways the system can be improved. [2:02:53] Beginners are encouraged to play around with the software, experiment with its capabilities, and actively work to make it better. [2:03:08]

Learning Through Open Source

For beginners who want to learn how to build software at a rapid pace, getting involved in open source is an essential strategy [2:03:19]. It is not necessary to focus on any one specific project; in fact, avoiding projects with massive backlogs might be more productive [2:03:30]. A great deal of knowledge can be gained simply by being humble and observing. Instead of rushing to send a pull request, beginners can learn by reading code, joining communities on platforms like Discord, and understanding the underlying architecture of how things are built [2:03:41]. A notable example is the community Mitchell Hashimoto has built around Ghostty, a terminal project that provides a rich environment for learning [2:04:04].

The Value of Programming Knowledge

There is a growing question of whether those who do not know how to program should still learn, given that natural language can now take a user quite far [2:04:15]. While agents are powerful, being able to read and write code from scratch remains highly beneficial [2:04:38]. It builds a level of intuition about the programming world that is hard to replicate otherwise [2:04:42]. That said, individuals with high agency and curiosity are finding they can achieve a lot even without deep technical backgrounds [2:04:54]. Because AI agents are infinitely patient, they allow users to ask endless questions to bridge their knowledge gaps.

Shifting from Engineer to Builder

A significant shift in mindset is required for modern developers, such as those transitioning from specialized roles like iOS engineering [2:05:08]. Instead of identifying as an engineer for a specific platform, it is more effective to see oneself as a builder [2:05:20]. General software construction knowledge can be applied to new domains, while agents handle the fine-grained details, such as array splicing or specific template syntax [2:05:34]. This flexibility makes it much easier to move between different technology stacks and ecosystems.

Pragmatic Choices in Programming Languages

Language selection is increasingly driven by the specific requirements of a project and how well the language integrates with AI agents [2:05:52]. Go is a prime example for building CLIs; while its syntax may not appeal to everyone, its ecosystem is excellent, it is garbage collected, and it offers significant speed [2:06:13]. It is a fascinating development that programmers are now using languages they might have previously avoided simply because LLMs are adept at generating resilient code for them [2:06:29].

The Future of the Agentic Ecosystem

When considering the best language for an agentic world, TypeScript is a strong candidate, though its ecosystem can be a "jungle" and its type systems can become overly complex [2:06:54]. As the industry moves toward a future where more code is generated, questions arise about what programming will look like in 20 to 40 years [2:07:26]. There is even a possibility of developing languages specifically designed for agents rather than humans [2:07:32]. One concern is that innovation might stagnate if new tools are not part of an agent's world knowledge, making established technologies easier to use by default [2:07:50].

Native Integration and Performance

For developers building Mac applications, Swift and SwiftUI are often chosen for their deep system integration [2:08:05]. The user experience of a native app is noticeably superior to that of an Electron app, which often feels like a web view loaded into a menu [2:08:19]. For those focused on high performance, Zig is an increasingly relevant language [2:08:32]. AI agents have improved drastically in their handling of Zig over the last six months, making it a viable option even though its ecosystem is still young [2:08:46]. Ultimately, the strength of a language's ecosystem remains a primary consideration for most developers [2:08:54].

Choosing the Right Tool for the Problem Domain

[2:09:00] For tasks involving inference or the direction of running models, Python remains a very good choice. However, if the goal is to build something that can be easily deployed on Windows, Python is often not the ideal selection. In some instances, after finding projects that achieved 90% of a desired goal but were written in Python, the solution was to simply rewrite them in Go to ensure a smooth Windows story.

[2:09:28] When the requirements move toward multiple threads and significantly higher performance, Rust becomes a really good choice. There is no single answer to language selection, which is part of the beauty of development. It is now possible to pick the language that has the most fitting characteristics and ecosystem for a specific problem domain. While reading the code in a new language might initially be slower, a builder can pick things up quickly by asking an agent for assistance.

Success Metrics and the Reality of Burnout

[2:10:19] Many builders look for advice on what metrics to optimize for, whether it is happiness, money, or positive impact. The journey often involves achieving these things but sometimes falling out of love with programming for a time. This can happen from "burning too bright for too long." For example, running PSPDFKit for 13 years was a high-stress experience that required learning how to manage people, bring on team members, and handle customers very quickly.

[2:11:17] Burnout is often not caused by working too much, though everyone is different. Instead, it can stem from the "people stuff," such as conflicts with co-founders or high-stress situations with customers that eventually grind a person down. After working for two years to make himself obsolete within the company, a successful exit became possible when a good offer arrived to take the business to the next level.

Finding Meaning After the Exit

[2:12:05] Upon leaving, there was a period where the "mojo" was simply gone. It felt impossible to get code out anymore, resulting in a sense of emptiness while staring at the screen. This led to a complete stop and a one-way trip to Madrid to catch up on life. It is not recommended to simply work hard with the goal of retiring early.

[2:13:13] Enjoying life is often most possible when there is a challenge to look forward to in the morning. Waking up with no real challenge can become boring very fast. Boredom often leads people to look for other ways to stimulate themselves, which can sometimes lead down dark paths toward substances.

A Philosophy on Money and Social Connection

[2:14:20] In building a company, money was never the primary driving force but rather felt like an affirmation of doing something right. While having money solves many problems, there are diminishing returns as wealth increases. Over-optimizing for luxury, such as private jets and exclusively luxury travel, can lead to a disconnection from society.

[2:15:00] Maintaining a connection to humanity is vital because humans are inherently interesting. This philosophy led to the creation of a foundation to help those who were not as lucky. Even when able to afford the finest hotels, choosing an "original" Airbnb experience by booking just a room in San Francisco helps maintain that connection to the world. [2:15:34] Being out among people provides a different perspective than being isolated in luxury.

Optimizing for Experiences

[2:15:34] Choosing a simple room in San Francisco over a luxury hotel was driven by a desire for a different experience. Life is ultimately about experiences, and tailoring one's life toward that goal reduces the need for every moment to be categorized as strictly good or bad. If you only seek good experiences, the strategy is fragile, but if you optimize for experiences themselves, everything becomes valuable. A good experience is amazing, and a bad experience is also amazing because you learned, saw, or did something new.

[2:16:12] During that stay, there was a queer DJ in the house. A bond was immediately formed by showing her how to make music using cloud code. This type of interaction is what makes the original couch-surfing or Airbnb model so powerful. It is about the variety and diversity of human connection. Travel remains awesome even when it is "shitty"—when it rains, when you are soaked, or when planes and plans are completely broken—because it creates emotion and feelings that make you feel alive.

The Lossiness of the Digital World

[2:16:55] Even interactions with difficult or cryptic people have value because they provoke emotion. However, the online experience currently lacks the intensity and "awesomeness" of real life. This is an open problem: how to infuse the cyber experience with the same intensity humans feel in person. Part of the issue is that text is extremely lossy. Ideally, an agent should be multi-modal so that it can understand the user's emotions. While we are not there yet, the technology will certainly move in that direction.

The Entrepreneurial Crossroads

[2:17:49] The sudden explosion of this project has opened many doors, including interest from almost every major venture capital (VC) firm. There is a "butterfly effect" moment happening where several paths are possible. One option is to do nothing and simply continue with life as it is, which is a valid choice that was briefly considered during a moment of wanting to delete the entire project. Another path is to create a new company.

[2:18:58] While starting a company could lead to raising hundreds of millions or even billions of dollars, the prospect is not particularly exciting. Having been a CEO before, there is an understanding that the role takes a significant amount of time away from the things that are actually enjoyable. Furthermore, starting a company creates a natural conflict of interest. It forces a prioritization of a "safe for work" version and creates dilemmas over features. If a contributor submits a pull request for an audit log, the owner might feel tempted to withhold it from the open-source version to sell it as an enterprise feature.

The Economics of Open Source

[2:20:13] Maintaining a project that is free as in beer—meaning free without conditions—is difficult. Changing the license to something like the Functional Source License (FSL) to restrict commercial use is complicated once many people have already contributed. Most companies struggle to make money while keeping their core project free. Even Tailwind, which is used by almost everyone, reportedly had to reduce its staff by 75% because they weren't generating enough revenue. As agents begin to handle more tasks, fewer people visit actual websites, which further erodes traditional monetization models.

[2:21:00] Relying on donations for a project of this caliber is not sustainable. Currently, the project is actually losing money. A point has been made to use sponsorship funds to support every dependency that is maintained by individuals. A company like Slack is large enough to manage without support, but the individual developers behind other dependencies deserve the backing. If more funds were available, the goal would be to buy merchandise for the contributors, but at this stage, the project remains financially unsustainable.

The financial reality of the project involves monthly costs between 10,000 and 20,000 dollars. While OpenAI has provided some assistance with tokens and other companies have been generous, the project continues to lose money. This situation has led to the consideration of several future paths, including potential partnerships with major AI labs. Among those currently being discussed, Meta and OpenAI have emerged as the most interesting candidates.

The Requirement for Open Source

Any agreement with a larger entity would require the project to remain open source. A potential structure could follow the Chrome and Chromium model, ensuring that the work remains a public resource. This technology is considered too significant to become the proprietary property of a single corporation.

The community energy surrounding the project is a major factor in this decision. At ClawCon in San Francisco, the level of inspiration and excitement was reminiscent of the early days of the internet, roughly 10 to 15 years ago. The event attracted high-caliber individuals and featured creative projects like robots in lobster suits. While the constant requests for selfies were a sensory challenge, the environment of hacking and learning is something that must be preserved.

Scaling into the Year of Personal Agents

The current year is expected to be the year of personal agents, and the most effective way to reach a broad audience is by partnering with an established lab. On a personal level, there is also an interest in experiencing the inner workings of a large company. Having never worked in such an environment, the opportunity to gain that perspective is valuable.

While some may view a potential partnership as "selling out," such a move would actually provide more resources for the project. The labs understand that this work has accelerated the development timeline and fostered genuine public interest in AI.

Product Validation and the Austrian Case Study

The potential of the project was demonstrated through a non-technical friend in Austria named Vahan. Despite his lack of programming knowledge, he became deeply engaged after OpenClaw was installed on his computer via WSL Windows. Initially, a subscription for Entropic costing between 90 and 100 dollars was provided for him. Within days, he was building his own tools and was so impressed that he upgraded to a 200 euro subscription.

However, Entropic blocked his account shortly thereafter due to their rules regarding subscription usage. This decision was viewed as shortsighted, as it alienated a highly engaged customer who was willing to pay a premium. Following the block, he moved to Mini Max for 10 dollars a month. This incident highlights the problems that arise when companies lock down their products too strictly during these early stages.

Exploration and the Future Format

The field of AI is currently in an era of exploration. It is similar to the early days of television when programming consisted of radio shows being filmed for a screen. We have yet to develop a modern TV show that fully utilizes the specific capabilities of the AI format. Most major labs acknowledge this, maintaining internal discussions about the experimental nature of the current landscape.

The goal is to keep demonstrating the possibilities of AI to non-technical users and to ensure that the development is guided by those with the right spirit and intentions. This project, which was built in just three months alongside other ventures, has already succeeded in showing many people what is possible. In January, this was just beginning to take shape.

my main focus because I saw the storm coming. Before that, I had built a whole bunch of other things. I have so many ideas; some should be realized now, while others would be much better fitted for when I have access to the latest toys. I really want to have access to those tools. This work is important and will continue to exist, but my short-term focus is working through a massive backlog of pull requests. I am not even sure of the exact count, perhaps 3,000 PRs by now. This is not the thing I am going to work on until I am 80. It is a window into the future, and I am going to turn it into a cool product, but I have more ideas beyond this.

Choosing Between Meta and OpenAI

[2:29:36] Deciding whether to lean toward Meta or OpenAI is incredibly difficult. A few weeks ago, I had not even considered any of this. It is hard because I know no one at OpenAI, yet I love their technology. I have been the biggest unpaid advertisement shill for Codex, and it would feel gratifying to finally put a price on all the work I have done for free. I would honestly love it if those companies just merged.

[2:30:32] This has been one of the hardest decisions I have ever had to make, reaching the same level of difficulty as major relationship breakups I have had in the past. In the end, I know that both companies are amazing and I cannot go wrong. They are prestigious, and they both truly understand scale. If you are thinking about impact and how to deploy technology securely and at scale to help a large number of people, both organizations understand that deeply.

Direct Engagement and Technical Credibility

[2:31:19] One of the most revealing aspects of this process was how the leadership interacted with the product. Both Ned and Mark Zuckerberg basically played with my product all week and sent me feedback. They would tell me when something was great or when it was "shit" and needed to change. Seeing people actually use your stuff is the biggest compliment, and it showed me that they actually care about it.

[2:31:47] I did not get that same experience on the OpenAI side, but I did get to see other things that I find really cool. While I cannot disclose exact numbers due to an NDA, they lured me with the promise of speed. If you consider the Cerebras deal and how that translates into speed, it was very intriguing. It felt like being given Thor's hammer. I have essentially been lured with tokens.

[2:32:34] The interaction with Mark Zuckerberg was particularly interesting. When he first approached me on WhatsApp and asked when we could have a call, I told him I do not like calendar entries and suggested we call right then. He asked for ten minutes because he needed to finish coding. That gave him immediate street cred; it showed he hasn't drifted away into being just a manager and that he "gets" me. Our first conversation was a 10-minute fight about whether Cloud Code or Codex was better. You do not usually have a casual 10-minute argument about that with someone who owns one of the largest companies in the world. Afterwards, I think he called me eccentric but brilliant.

[2:33:30] I also had some really cool discussions with Sam Altman. He is very thoughtful and brilliant, and I liked him a lot from the little time I had with him. I know some people vilify both of these individuals, but I do not think that is fair. I am super pumped about the future. The beauty is that if it doesn't work out, I can just do my own thing again. I told them both that I do not do this for the money. I want to have fun and have impact, and that is ultimately what made my decision.

Implementing the Agentic Loop

[2:34:58] To zoom out on how OpenCloud works, there are several distinct components: the gateway, the chat clients, the harness, and the agentic loop. I have said before that everybody should implement an agent loop at some point in their lives. It is a fundamental part of the process because it is like the...

Implementing an agent loop is essentially the Hello World of AI. It is actually quite simple and serves as a vital exercise for understanding that this technology is not magic; you can easily build it yourself. I even led a practice at a conference in Paris to introduce people to AI by having them write their own little cloud code.

Proactivity and the Heartbeat Mechanism

One idea that turned out to be quite effective was building a system with full system access. With great power comes great responsibility, so I looked for ways to increase the stakes. I made the system proactive by adding a prompt. Initially, it was a simple instruction to surprise me every half hour. Over time, I refined the definition of what constitutes a surprise to be more specific.

The fact that the system is proactive, knows you, and is prompted to care about you makes the experience very interesting. Because it follows your current session, it might occasionally ask a follow-up question or check in with a prompt like, "How's your day?" While this can feel a little creepy or weird, it is also fascinating. This proactive behavior is driven by what we call Heartbeat, a mechanism that regularly kicks off the agentic loop.

Critics often dismiss this idea by saying it is just a cron job. However, you can reduce almost any idea to something silly. It is similar to the criticism that Dropbox is just FTP with extra steps, or the reductionist view that love is merely evolutionary biology manifesting itself. While the project is technically a collection of glue and dependencies, its originality and value lie in the implementation.

Relatability Through Context

The model does not always choose to use the Heartbeat function frequently. For example, I had a shoulder operation a few months ago. While the model rarely used the Heartbeat feature normally, it knew I was in the hospital and checked up on me, asking, "Are you okay?" When something significant exists in the context, it triggers that proactive behavior. This makes the AI feel significantly more relatable to the user.

The Skill Hub and the MCP Debate

The architecture of OpenCloud includes a local agent runtime, the gateway, chat clients, a harness, the agentic loop, and a growing set of skills within the Skill Hub. Half a year ago, there was significant hype surrounding MCPs (Model Context Protocol). My stance was that every MCP would be better implemented as a CLI (Command Line Interface).

OpenCloud does not have core support for MCPs, and users have not complained. My approach is that if you want to extend a model with more features, you should build a CLI. The model can call the CLI, and if it gets the command wrong, it can call the help menu. It then loads what it needs into the context on demand. A model only needs a single sentence to know a CLI exists if it is not something the model knows about by default.

Skills are perfect for this because they boil down to a single sentence that explains the capability. The model then loads the skill, which explains the CLI, and the model uses that CLI. While some skills are raw, most function through these network-like interactions.

Structured Protocols versus Procedural Skills

According to Perplexity, there is a distinct difference between MCPs and skills. MCPs are more structured protocols for communicating with APIs, databases, or files. In contrast, skills are more about how the model should work, involving procedures, helper scripts, and prompts often written in semi-structured natural language.

Technically, skills could replace MCPs if the model is smart enough. The primary advantage is that models are already exceptionally good at calling Unix commands. So if you just add another...

The Composability of CLIs vs. MCPs

[2:41:07] A significant advantage of models is their inherent ability to call Unix commands. A CLI is ultimately just another Unix command, which is a very natural interface for a model to interact with. In contrast, the Model Context Protocol (MCP) often requires specific syntax that must be addressed during model training, making it less intuitive.

The most substantial issue with MCP is that it is not composable. For example, if a service provides a large data blob containing temperature, average temperature, rain, wind, and other metrics, the model is forced to ingest that entire "huge blob" into its context before it can pick out the specific information it needs. There is no natural way for the model to filter that data unless the developer proactively builds filtering logic into the MCP itself.

If the same service were built as a CLI, the model could simply add a JQ command to filter the output and retrieve only the necessary data. It could even compose a script to perform calculations—such as processing temperature data—and return only the exact result. This approach prevents context pollution. While workarounds like sub-agents exist to handle large data structures, they are often less optimal than the direct composability of CLI tools.

The Problem with Context Clutter

[2:42:27] MCPs were beneficial because they encouraged companies to build APIs, which can now be converted into CLIs. However, the inherent problem remains: by default, MCPs tend to clutter the model's context. Furthermore, because many MCPs are not well-constructed, the paradigm is often not very useful.

There are exceptions, such as Playwright, which is used for browser automation. Because Playwright requires state management, it is an acceptable and useful choice for providing a model with browser capabilities.

Apps as Slow APIs and the Rise of Personal Agents

[2:43:17] In the current landscape, every application can be viewed as a very slow API, whether the developers intend it to be or not. Through the use of personal agents, many traditional apps may eventually disappear.

An example of this shift is a CLI built for Twitter (now X) called Bird. It functioned by reverse-engineering the internal API of the website. While the platform has since made access more difficult, they haven't actually removed the possibility of access; they have simply made it slower. If an agent wants to read a tweet and the API is restricted, the agent can still open a browser and read it. It doesn't matter if a service wants to be an API; if it can be accessed via a browser, it is effectively a "slow API."

Balancing Developer Needs and Platform Protection

[2:44:40] Platforms like X restrict API access to protect against large-scale scraping by other major companies. However, this often cuts off helpful use cases for smaller developers. A potential solution would be to allow a low per-day baseline for each account, specifically for read-only access.

There are many valuable automations possible with this kind of access. For instance, a user could create a bookmark on X, and then use a tool like OpenClaw to find that bookmark, perform research on the topic, and send an email with a detailed summary. Many users find high-quality content on X and bookmark it, but they rarely look back at those bookmarks. Tooling that organizes and researches these bookmarks would be an incredible application.

AI Smell and Automation Policies

[2:45:51] While there is a clear need for better developer access, there is also a strong stance against AI-driven automation on social platforms. Using AI to generate tweets often results in a recognizable "AI smell" that lacks a completely human touch.

A zero-tolerance policy for AI-generated tweets is one approach to maintaining platform quality, including blocking accounts that use AI to interact. To manage this, it would be helpful if tweets made via an API were clearly marked. Additionally, there should be a straightforward, official way for agents to obtain their own dedicated accounts on platforms like Twitter to ensure transparency and proper integration.

Rethinking Social Platforms for an Agent-Driven Future

[2:47:07] There is a pressing need to rethink social platforms as we move toward a future where every individual has their own digital agent. These agents might have their own Instagram profiles or Twitter accounts to perform tasks on a user's behalf. It is crucial that such activity is clearly marked so users know when they are interacting with an agent rather than the person themselves.

As content becomes increasingly inexpensive to produce, human attention becomes the most valuable and expensive resource. Encountering content that "smells" like AI can be a triggering experience for many. [2:47:41] This raises questions about the future value of the human experience. There may be a significant shift toward in-person interactions as people talk to AI agents to accomplish tasks or learn, while devaluing online interactions due to the prevalence of "AI slop" and bots.

The Value of Raw Humanity and the "AI Smell"

[2:48:15] If filtering technology is smart enough, it should not be difficult to manage this influx of AI content, allowing users to choose when they want to engage with it. Currently, many people receive emails that are "agentically written"—meaning they were generated by an AI agent. There is a growing preference for reading broken English or even seeing typos over polished AI-generated text. A prompt often contains more of the human's intent than the resulting AI output.

[2:49:01] Even when attempting to steer an agent to match a specific writing style, the results often miss the necessary nuances. While AI can be useful for fixing minor typos, there is profound value in the rough parts of human expression. [2:49:53] This realization suggests that AI might actually lead us to value raw humanity more deeply.

Selective Acceptance of AI Tools

[2:50:02] There is a notable distinction in where AI is accepted. While AI is highly valued for writing code or technical documentation, there is an almost allergic reaction to it when used for storytelling or visual media. [2:50:17] AI-generated video, images, and infographics can quickly feel like "slop." While these were novel for a short period, they now often trigger a negative response, making the content seem less credible.

[2:51:06] Creating high-quality, hallucination-free diagrams with AI actually requires a significant amount of manual labor. Without that effort, AI diagrams can feel "fake" or "fraudulent," similar to the way many people view the Comic Sans font. [2:51:35] This "smell" of AI is a reminder that humans have an innate ability to recognize genuine human effort. This provides hope that the human experience will be empowered by AI tools rather than damaged or limited by them.

Agents and the Obsolescence of Traditional Apps

[2:52:24] The rise of agents may fundamentally transform the app market, potentially making many existing applications obsolete. [2:52:42] For instance, a dedicated app like MyFitnessPal may become unnecessary if an agent already knows a user's location. If an agent sees a user is at a Waffle House or a brisket spot in Austin, it can infer the user's dietary choices. [2:53:04] Furthermore, an agent could automatically modify a gym workout based on data it has regarding the user's sleep quality or stress levels, integrating information that currently lives in separate, disconnected apps.

Whether a user is experiencing stress or not, an agent possesses significantly more context to make superior decisions [2:53:12] compared to what any individual app can provide. An agent can display a user interface exactly as the user prefers it, raising the question of why a dedicated app is even necessary. There is little reason to pay for another subscription for a service that an agent can now perform autonomously.

For instance, a user should not need an Eight Sleep app to control their bed when the agent already understands the user's location and habits [2:53:34]. The agent can simply turn off devices that are not in use. This shift will likely lead to an entire category of apps that people naturally stop using because agents perform the tasks more effectively [2:53:47].

It is estimated that this transformation could potentially eliminate 80% of existing apps [2:54:00]. Such a change would have a massive impact on the software development industry and could lead to the closure of many software companies. While this is a significant economic shift, it also empowers users to accomplish tasks more efficiently and at a lower cost [2:54:34].

New Service Categories and Agent Allowances

As old apps disappear, new types of services will emerge. A user might want to give their agent a specific allowance, such as $100, to solve problems [2:54:41]. If the agent is instructed to order food, it might utilize a service like rent-a-human to ensure the task is completed. The primary concern for the user is the resolution of the problem, not the specific tool used.

Some companies may survive by transforming their products into APIs specifically designed for agent interaction [2:55:13]. Services like Uber Eats have an opportunity to be first-movers by creating the most natural and easiest ways to interact with platforms like OpenClaw. Apps will likely become APIs whether they intend to or not, because agents are learning to navigate phones and click buttons on behalf of the user [2:55:50].

The Shift Toward Data and APIs

On Android, agents can already navigate the interface to click an "Order Uber" button [2:56:06]. In the future, specialized APIs may make these actions even faster. This represents a new frontier in software that is only beginning to be understood. Access to data is becoming the most critical factor.

Many existing apps are poorly designed, but if they have an API, the agent can bypass the app entirely [2:56:34]. For example, instead of using a "crappy" app for Sonos speakers or security cameras, an agent can communicate with the hardware directly through an API. This forces companies to rethink their business models and how they generate revenue, similar to the transition seen during the early days of the internet [2:57:01].

Navigating the Developer Jungle

Some major companies make direct access difficult. Because there is no official CLI for Google, a tool called GAWK was built to provide a CLI for Google services [2:57:10]. Accessing Gmail data as a company is an incredibly complicated process, sometimes taking six months for certification. Startups have even been known to acquire other startups just to inherit their Google certification status.

However, an individual user's agent can access Gmail because it connects through the user's own account [2:57:51]. While navigating the Google developer jungle to get an API key is annoying, companies cannot easily prevent this access. In the worst-case scenario, the agent can simply browse the website and extract the data it needs.

The Battle Against Bot Detection

Agents are now capable of navigating browsers and even clicking "I'm not a robot" buttons [2:58:18]. This is leading to an escalating conflict with companies like Cloudflare that attempt to prevent bot access. While bot prevention is useful for stopping mass scraping, it can be a hindrance for personal users who want their agents to interact with the web [2:58:28].

If a user tries to read a Medium article using an agent but the site blocks access, the user is forced to copy and paste the text manually [2:58:43]. Over time, users may simply stop visiting websites that are not agent-friendly, favoring platforms that allow for seamless AI interaction. Powerful, wealthy companies will likely fight back against this trend, placing developers and users at the center of a major revolution in how we interact with digital services [2:59:13].

Resistance from Established Platforms

[2:59:31] The evolution of the web and the way we interact with services will face significant pushback from major corporations. Companies like Google and other industry giants are likely to resist these changes because they disrupt established business models. Currently, many users are already shifting toward alternative providers like Perplexity or Brave because Google does not make it easy to access its information without using its specific platform. It is a strategy that remains to be seen if it will succeed in the long term.

[2:59:58] There is a delicate balance for large companies during a technological revolution. If an organization pushes back too hard for too long, they risk becoming like Blockbuster, eventually losing everything to more adaptable competitors like Netflix. While some resistance is expected as companies navigate this shift, they must ultimately listen to what the public wants.

The Shift Toward Agentic Connectivity

[3:00:11] What users truly desire is a more fluid and connected experience. For someone on the go, the ideal is not to manually open a calendar app to set a reminder for a dinner tomorrow night, nor is it to navigate through separate menus to invite friends or send a WhatsApp message. We have moved past the era where opening individual apps for every discrete task is efficient or desirable.

[3:00:40] Everything is becoming much more connected and fluid, whether these companies want it or not. The organizations that find ways to adapt and jump on this train will survive, while others that refuse to evolve may perish. The future belongs to those who facilitate this seamless interaction rather than blocking it.

The Evolution of Programming and the Builder Identity

[3:00:55] A significant concern for many developers today is whether AI will completely replace human programmers. In many ways, the industry is moving in that direction. Programming is ultimately just one part of building a product. While AI may eventually handle the core task of writing code, there is a much deeper art to creation that remains relevant.

[3:01:30] This art involves deciding what to build, how it should feel to the user, and how the overall architecture is designed. Agents may not replace these high-level creative and structural decisions. The actual act of programming might stay, but it may transform into something akin to knitting—a craft that people pursue because they enjoy it, rather than because it is the most practical way to achieve a result.

Mourning the Craft and Redefining Flow

[3:01:59] It is natural for developers to mourn the loss of their traditional craft. Many have spent years tinkering and finding deep satisfaction in the state of flow that comes from cranking out code and discovering beautiful solutions. There is an understandable sadness in seeing that specific experience fade into the past.

[3:02:43] However, a similar state of flow can be achieved by working with agents and thinking deeply about complex problems. While the experience is different, the intellectual challenge remains. For a long time, there was a lack of intelligence available for building things, which drove software developer salaries to exceptionally high levels. As tokenized intelligence enables people to do more and work faster, these economic and professional dynamics will shift.

Historical Parallels and the Identity of a Builder

[3:03:49] This transition is reminiscent of the Industrial Revolution and the creation of the steam engine. When factories replaced manual labor, people revolted and broke the machines. It is understandable that those who deeply identify as programmers feel threatened when the work they love and excel at is performed by an external entity.

[3:04:31] However, viewing oneself strictly as a programmer is a limiting perspective. Developers are, first and foremost, builders. The transition that occurred between April and November represents a massive leap that has been painful for those who have spent thousands of hours poring over code in editors like Emacs.

[3:05:21] It is a significant and painful shift to have an identity and a source of meaning replaced in just a few months. Yet, the core essence of being a builder remains. Programmers are generally at their best when they are solving problems and creating, regardless of the specific tools used to translate those ideas into reality.

equipped at this moment in history to learn the language, to empathize with agents, and to learn the language of agents. There is a particular skill in being able to feel the CLI and understand exactly what the agent needs to perform a task to the best of its ability.

At some point, this process is likely just going to be called coding again, and it will simply be the new normal. Even when not writing the code manually, one can still feel very much in the driver's seat, as if they are writing the code themselves. The identity of being a programmer remains; it is just the specific activity of a programmer that is becoming different.

Reactions to the Technological Shift

The reception to these changes depends heavily on the platform. On X, the bubble is mostly positive, but on Mastodon and Bluesky, there can be stronger reactions and personal attacks in response to blog posts. While it is unfair for people to unload their fear and hate onto an individual, it is possible to sympathize with those reactions because this change is significant. [3:07:15] It is going to be a challenging transition, but it is also incredibly fun and gratifying.

This shift allows builders to use their time to focus on much more specific details. The level of expectation for what is built is rising because the default state of software creation is now so much easier. Software is changing in many ways, and there is going to be a lot more of it.

Addressing Environmental Concerns

When people raise concerns about the environmental impact, such as the water use of data centers, it is helpful to look at the actual mathematics. [3:08:34] For most people, skipping just one burger patty per month compensates for the CO2 output or the water use equivalent of a significant amount of tokens.

While the math is tricky—and pre-training might make the cost higher than a single patty—it is not off by a factor of a hundred. Furthermore, activities like golf still use significantly more water than all data centers combined. It is common for critics to grab onto anything they perceive as bad about AI without seeing the potential things that might be good, though it is certain that this will be a very transformative technology for society.

The Silicon Valley Bubble and Human Impact

To steel man the criticism of this movement, there is a recognized bubble within Silicon Valley where there is an over-focus on the positive potential of technology. [3:09:51] Within that excitement, there can be a dismissal of the basic human experience across the United States, the Midwest, and the rest of the world.

This includes the programmers who may lose their jobs and the measurable pain and suffering that happens on a short-term scale whenever there is a large-scale transformative change. There needs to be a level of humility and awareness that the tools being built will cause pain. While they may bring about a better world and more opportunities in the long term, there should be a moment of respect for the pain that will be felt during the transition.

Accessibility and Empowerment through OpenClaw
